title: aga
snippet:
gaagaagaagannnn `zealous (child, pupil)'. 
Attested only once, in a late mediaeval song [NHB 1: 2c]: Zi sireli ic`es mardkan, 
/ Ler yusaneld manuk agan! "Be zealous in your study so that you be loved by 
people". 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Clackson (1994: 223-22498) ascribes a meaning `early' to agan and identifies 
it to -agan found in anagan `late; evening (time)' (q.v.). The latter is considered, thus, 
as composed of the privative prefix an- and agan `early', literally *`not-early'. This 
is actually proposed first in NHB 1: 101a. However, in its only attestation (see 
above) agan means, as stated by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 75a), `zealous (child, pupil)' 
rather than `early'. Therefore, the connection with an-agan is possible only in terms 
of a semantic development `early' > `quick(-minded)' > `zealous, diligent'.

title: aznavur
snippet:
rbBar’īć 1, 2001: 8a]. The -h- instead of -w- 
is also seen in *anjnahur (see below). 
In the meaning `nobleman': S’īīatax a’īłzna’īłvur [M. Muradyan 1962: 208a]; Akn 
aznawur (as a personal-name) [Gabrie’īälean 1912: 233]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM According to AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 87b), Arm. azn `generation, nation, tribe' (cf. 
azn-iw `noble' in Bible+) has been borrowed into Georg. aznauri `nobleman' and 
from Georgian re-borrowed into Arm. dial. *aznawor `huge man, giant; supernatural 
being, spirit'. Given the facts that in most of the dialects Arm. *azn-awor is not 
semantically identic with Georg. aznauri `nobleman', and is widespread in Armenian 
dialects most of them being geographicall y very far from Georgia, and the suffix 
-awor is very productive in Armenian , the interpretation of Arm. *azn-awor as a 
Georgian loan is not probable.

title: alawunk
snippet:
see 3.1.1-2, 3.1.4. 
In "Bar’īćgirk` hayoc`" (see Amalyan 1975: 8Nr128), alawun, var. alasun, is rendered 
by bazmast/lmidtilde or bazum ast/lmidtilde or erroneously bazmata/lmidtilde (cf. HAB 1: 9, 92a) `Pleiades'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM J’īīahukyan (1963a: 86; cf. 1987: 270, with some reservation) connects to 
a/lmidtildeawni `dove' deriving both from *al╔Öu- `white, shiny' and comparing also *albho-, 
read *h2elbho-. This etymology seems to me uncertain, since the only (cited) 
evidence for *-╔Öu-n- is taken from the Celtic onomastics, and there are no strong 
semantic parallels. One might reformulate the connection, deriving alaw(s)unk` 
directly from a/lmidtildeawni, regardless of the ultimate origin of the latter. However, neither 
this would be convincing because, firstly: -l- instead of -/lmidtilde- is not explained. 
Secondly, the origin of -s- remains obscure. Thirdly, a/lmidtildeawni `dove' is a derivation 
with -i suffix, but the expected (folketymological) development would be `dove' > 
`star' and not the other way around. Finally, to the best of my knowledge, in 
Armenian tradition, unlike in that of Greek (cf. Scherer 1953: 144; Puhvel 1991: 
1244), the Pleiades are never interpreted as doves.

title: acem
snippet:
de, Agulis, J’īīu/lmidtildea, etc.): `to pour', `to play a music 
instrument' [HAB 1: 102]. See also s.v. acu `garden-bad'. On the epenthetic -r- in 
*arcu `garden-bed' and *arceli (vs. ac-eli) `razor' see 2.1.30.2. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Windischmann and Gosche, connected with Skt. a’īüjati, Av. azaiti, Gr. 
’üĪ’éö’ü¦’üĘ `to lead', Lat. ago, etc. [Hu’īłbschmann 1896: 412 Nr6; HAB 1: 101-102] : PIE 
*h2eg^- `to drive, lead'. 
Given the absence of the initial h- as the expected reflex of the laryngeal, 
Clackson (1994: 218 3) points out: "Kortlandt's rule that *h2e- goes to Armenian ha- 
does not explain acem `I bring'". In fact, Kortlandt (2003 [< 1983 and 1996]: 44, 
118; see also Beekes 2003: 175, 182) derived acem from *h2g^-es-, cf. Lat. gero’īä `to 
bring' (on which see Schrijver 1991: 18-19); see also Greppin 1983: 263. 
Considering this etymology problematic, Clackson (2004-05: 155) prefers to connect 
acem with the widespread thematic present *h2eg^e/o- and suggests that the initial h- 
might have been lost "through influence from compound words ending in -ac, which 
were synchronically associated with the verb acem (Olsen 1999:231-6)". 
The meaning `to play a music instrument' is derivable from `to beat, sling' (cf. 
Skt. aj- `to drive, sling', go-a’īüjana- 'whip, stick for driving cattle', Arm. gawazan `id.'

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM A derivative of acem `to bring; to lead; to move; etc.' (q.v.) < PIE *h2eg^-: 
Skt. a’īüjati, Gr. ’üĪ’éö’ü¦’üĘ `lead' (Il.), etc. [HAB 1: 101-102]. Arm. acu is directly compared 
with Gr. ’üĪ’éö’ü¦’üĄ’ü®’üĪ , pl. ’üĪ’Ć¦’ü¦’üĄ’ü®’üĪ’ü® ’Ć» f. `street, road' (Il.) and interpreted as perfect participle 
*-us-ieh2- (see J’īīahukyan 1987: 241; cf. Clackson 1994: 225124). 
After a thorough examination of the Greek word, however, Szemere’īünyi (1964: 
206-208) concludes: "It seems theref ore clear that the connection of ’üĪ’éö’ü¦’üĄ’ü®’üĪ and ’üĪ’éö’ü¦’üĘ 
is nothing more than popular etymology, probably overlying and obscuring an 
indigenous word". See also Beekes 1998: 25 and his Database (s.v.). How to explain, 
then, the similar pattern seen in Armenian acem `to lead' : acu `garden-bed', which 
are not mentioned in this context? Whatever the exact details of their origin and 
development, the Greek and Armenian words under discussion seem to belong to 
each other. 
The hypothetical development of the paradigm would be as follows: NSg. 
*ag^us-ih2- > PArm. *acu’īü-i > ClArm. acu, NPl *ag^us-ih2-es > *acu-i-k`, oblique 
*ag^us-ieh2- > PArm. *acu-ia-

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Arm. akanj’īī(-k`) is originally the dual of unkn `ear' (q.v.), and the j’īī is treated

dual *-’ē¦. Others directly posit *-n-ih1, without the velar between *n and *i (see 
Greppin 1983: 264 and Lindeman 1982: 39 for references; cf. also Winter 1986: 
22-23). Note that *h2(e)u’ī»s-n-ih1 (cf. e.g. Eichner 1978: 14717, 151) would yield Arm. 
*(h)aganj’īī. The same holds for *╔Ö3ws-nt-y╔Ö1 [= *h3ws-nt-ih1] reconstructed by 
Witczak (1999: 175). Lindeman (1980; 1982: 39) assumes *awsn’īź-a (cf. Gr. ’ü»’üĄ’éö’üĪ’ü┤’üĪ < 
*owsn’īź-t-a) > Arm. *aw(h)an-a + -ŌĆ░` from aŌĆ░` `eye' with subsequent voicing after 
nasal. Arriving at *aganj’īī, he, basing himself upon the idea of voiced aspirates in 
Armenian, derives akanj’īī from *aganjh < *aghanjh through dissimilation of aspirates. 
For other proposals/references see J’īīahukyan 1982: 22260.

I conclude that the word represents an isogloss involving the groups 6 and 7, as 
well as the eastern part of the group 2. This seems to be partly confirmed by the

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymological attempt is recorded in HAB. 
J’īīahukyan (1967: 151) lists akut` among words showing no consonant shift, 
linking it with the PIE word for `oven': *Hukw: OIc. ofn, Gr. ’ü®’Ć¦’ü░’ü«’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ , etc. Greppin 
(1983: 265) gives the entry between square brackets. The etymology is accepted in 
Mallory/Adams 1997: 443b. Here 
akut` is derived from the delabialized (after *-u-) 
variant *Huk-: Lat. aulla `pot', Goth. au’īühns `oven', Skt. ukha’īü `cooking pot'. 
However, this is very improbable since the formal problems are hard to surmount. 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 472) compares to Akkad. akuku’īätu `half-burnt wood', considering 
the resemblance as doubtful or accidental.

In favour of Abrahamyan's etymology one notes the following arguments: 1) the 
derivational suffix -uk fits in the interpretation; 2) the Armenian word is not attested 
in the Classical period and does not look old; 3) there are formal problems (one

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Hu’īłbschmann (1897: 416Nr17), am has been connected to Skt. sa’īüm’É░- f. 
`year, season'. The other forms have shifted the semantics to `summer': YAv. ham-; 
OIr. sam; etc; cf. s.v. amar’īćn. The semantic relationship between am `year' and amar’īćn 
`summer' is parallel to Russ. let : leto (cf. Sarad’å×eva 1986: 79, 88). The remarkable 
correspondence of the meaning and of the stems of the Armenian and the Sanskrit 
forms (cf. Tumanjan 1978: 204; S’īīirokov 1980: 82) should be explained as an 
archaism rather than a shared innovation fo r most of the cognates meaning `summer' 
are derivations [ *-r/n ?], and the direction of the semantic shift seems to be `year' > 
`summer', not the other way around. An old paradigm *s(e)m-eh2-/ *sm’īź-h2-o’īü- is 
restored, see 1981: 13; Mayr hofer, EWAia 2, 1996: 704; Olsen 1999: 60; cf. also 
Frisk 1944: 32 (= 1966: 280); Tumanjan 1978: 204. The initial a- is due to 
generalization of the oblique stem: PIE *sRHV- > Arm. *aRV- (compare Beekes 
1988: 78).

[1987: 512-549]. The reason for this might have been, I assume, the fact that the

(1990: 94-95; cf. 1991: 26) rejects AŌĆ░ar’īćyan's etymology, arguing that the Pahl. 
unattested *ama’īänak would yield, as AŌĆ░ar’īćyan himself notes, Pers. *ama’īäna, which 
does not exist. However, this is not a solid argument since, for instance, in the case 
of ’å×aman, ’å×amanak `time' Persian has both zama’īän and zama’īäna; cf. Pahl. zama’īän, 
zama’īänak [HAB 2: 222-223]. Further, Hovhannisyan assumes that amanak can be

`summer': YAv. ham-, Khot. hama’īäna-, MPers. ha’īäm’ē¦n, OIr. sam, OHG sumar. The 
suffixal element *-r- is present in Armenian and Germanic. The final -n of Armenian 
is explained from *-om (cf. Pokorny 1959: 905; J’īīahukyan 1967: 212; 1982: 115; 
1987: 147) or from an old IE accusative *smh2er-m [Kortlandt 1985: 21 Nr7]. The 
latter seems more attractive. I find the idea about the contamination of the two 
alternants of the original heteroclitic paradigm, i. e. *-r- and *-n(t)- (see Mayrhofer, 
KEWA 3, 1976: 437; Olsen 1999: 128, 141, 410, 855), hard to accept; cf. also 
Greppin 1983: 277: *sm’īź-r’īź-n-. 
Mentioning the plural forms of jmer’īćn `winter' and k`irtn `sweat' going back to *-
on(t)h2-, Olsen (1999: 128) writes: "No doubt amar’īćn `summer', which is accidentally 
not attested in the plural, is part of the same pattern". However, we do find an APl 
amaruns in Philo; see NHB 1: 52b.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB1: 156b) places the ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde form under Classical amik `a 
one-year-old male kid or lamb' (q. v.), which is obviously derived from Arm. am 
`year' (< IE *sm’īźH-), but then he adds that it seems to have been borrowed from 
Turk. emlik `sucking lamb'. I think this is not necessary since amlik can easily be 
derived from Arm. am with the suffixal element *-li(h2)- and diminutive -ik: *sm’īźH-l- 
> Arm *amal-ik > dial. amlik through syncope. An astonishing parallel is found in 
the Scandinavian words with a basic meaning `one-year-old animal', which are of 
the same origin: OIc. simull, Norw. simla, etc.; see Pokorny 1959: 905. This might 
be a late Indo-European innovation shared by Armenian and Ge rmanic (cf. another 
animal-name which certainly is an Armeno-Germanic isoglosse, that is tuar `cattle', 
q.v.), although one cannot perhaps exclude the possibility of independent 
developments. See s.v. am for more details; cf. also Gr. ’üż’üĪ’Ć»’üŁ -’üĪ’ü¼’ü®’ü¬ , ’üż’üĪ’üŁ -’üĪ’Ć»’ü¼’ü© `young

’äć’äć’äć’äć
ETYM The interpretation of the word as an-par `motionless' suggested by the same 
author is not accepted by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 163), who gives no comments. The 
inclusion of par into this etymology seems attractive since par refers to the 
movement of the stars, too (see NHB 1: 383b; 2: 625b), and Ali’åÜan himself is aware 
of that, cf. Ali’åÜan 1910: 118. However, the meaning `motionless' is the opposite of 
what one would use describing the planets. Note also 
anpar denoting persons who 
cannot dance properly, in Philo apud NHB 1: 229a. Thus, if ampar contains par, the 
first part of the word should be identified with the prefix am- or something else but 
not with the privative an-. 
I know of no other etymological proposals. 
As we have seen, the postulation of par is possible. Nevertheless, I alternatively 
propose a connection of ampar `planet' with amp `cloud' and amprop `thunder' 
(q.v.). In the first instance, the relation seems semantically unmotivated. However, 
one should bear in mind that some of the cognates both with and without *-ro- (Hitt. 
ne’īäpi’åÜ-, OCS nebo, Os. arv, etc) mean

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 165b) mentions many etymological proposals but does not 
accept any of them. Among them one should mention that of Patruba’īüny (1905: 158) 
which suggests a connection with Gr. ’üĪ’ü®’Ć¦’üĘ’Ć»’ü« `Leben(szeit), Zeit(dauer), lange Zeit', 
Skt. a’īä’īüyu- `lifetime', etc. The phonological development seems impeccable: PIE 
NSg*h2(e)i’ī»u’ī»-o’īän > Arm. *aygu(n) > ayg, -u, cf. LSg aygun `in the morning' (cf. 
Olsen 1999: 108222; the origin of -un is not specified). However, the semantics is not 
clear. Though the meanings `time' and `day' may relate to each other (cf. Arm. awr 
`day; (life)time' and, if cognate, OIr. amm `time'), I am not sure whether the 
direction `time' > `day' is probable. Besides, ayg means `morning' and not `day'. 
Thus, the etymology is uncertain. 
J’īīahukyan (XX) derived ayg from IE *ai- (= *h2ei-) `to burn, shine'. However, -g 
is unexplained. Later J’īīahukyan himself seems to doubt the etymology since he 
excludes the word from the list of the native words (1987: 111-157) and mentions it 
under a question-mark in p. 295 where he assumes hesitantly that Finnish

Av. ahu- m. `lord, overlord', Hitt. ßĖ½a’åÜ- `to procreate, give birth', PGerm. *ansuz 
`Gott, Ase', etc. For Armenian he assumes *ans-i’ī»o- (> ays, with regular loss of the 
sibilant before the nasal and with subsequent metathesis *asy- > ays), though this is 
not confirmed by any cognate form. Then he mentions the derivation of the PIE word 
from 
*h2enh1- `to breathe' (on this see e.g. Mallory/Adams 1997: 330b) and states 
that this is confirmed by the semantics of the Armenian word. On the other hand, 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 450) also mentions Arab. ßĖ½anzab `devil'. 
In the whole, the etymology is uncertain, but not impossible. 
One prefers positing *h2(e)nsu-i’ī»o- [Olsen 1999: 958], though the expected 
Armenian form seems to be *asu’īü(yo).

explanation as yet (see HAB 1: 179a; Olsen 1999: 938).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM The word is analysed as distinct from an-ari `uncourageous', which is 
undoubtedly correct, and is derived from the Iranian form of `non-Aryan', cf. YAv. 
anairiia-, Pahl. ane’īär `non-Aryan, ignoble' [HAB 1: 181-182]. Dume’īüzil (1997: 3-4) 
accepts this etymology and for the semantics compares Lat. in-gens `vast, huge' : 
"was unserem Geschlechte nicht zustimmt , daher u’īłber die Gro’īłsse und Art unseres 
Geschlechtes hinausgeht" (< Fick). 
I alternatively propose to treat an anari as an- + *ar- + -i, with the root *ar- that 
may be identic with Arm. *ar- seen in y-arm-ar `fitting', ar’īćnem (1SgAor arari) `to 
make; to create', y-arem `to put together', ard `shape', from PIE * h2er- `to fit'; cf. 
Gr. ’üĪ’Ć¦’ü▓’üĪ’ü▓’ü®’Ć»’ü│’ü½’üĘ `to fit together, construct, equip', etc. Thus, an-ar-i basically means 
`unshaped, deformed'; cf. an-ard-i(l), where *ar- is replaced by a derivative from the 
same ard-4.

snippet:
compare

a) thin /cattle/arj’īīar’īć/" in a fairy-tale [HZ’īīHek` 14, 999: 13-39] recorded in 1915 (p. 
16). 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM According to J’īīahukyan (1972: 308), belongs to IE *h2(e)ngwhi- `snake'; cf. 
s.v. awj. He does not give any detail. The connection seems to be formally 
satisfactory. The labiovelar is not palatalized because of the preceding nasal; cf. 
*penkwe > hing `five', etc. 
However, one has to account for the relationship between awj and *angi. The 
strange shape of the former is usually explained by the influence of the labiovelar, as 
in awcanem `to anoint'. This rule may have only functioned with the zero grade. The 
IE word under discussion displays forms with both full (Lith. angi’īĆs, OPruss. angis 
`snake') and zero (OHG unc `snake') grades, Lat. anguis `snake' and OIr. esc-ong 
`eel' (lit. `water-snake') being ambiguous (see Schrijver 1991: 43-44, 60). One may 
therefore reconstruct a HD i-stem: NSg. *h2e’īüngwh-o’īäi-, GSg. *h2ngwh-i-o’īüs. The 
theoretical PArm. paradigm would then be as follows: NSg. *(h)angu(i) > *ang-(i), 
GSg. *anwgiyo- > awji (= ClArm. GSg.). Then the genitive has been generalised 
(with a new nominative awj), while *ang-i has been preserved in ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde. Note

I hypothetically suggest a connection with Moks a’īŖngyu’īłt [Orbeli 2002: 202],

list). [Could it be from -oyt` ?]. The same root, viz. *ank- in ank-anim `to fall', has 
formed another synonym in the same dialect - ang(a)uk (see HayLezBrbBar’īć 1, 2001: 
56b), with the suffix -uk. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymological attempt is known to me. 
I think angti may be derived from ankanim / anganim `to fall down', which also 
means `to sin, prostitute' already in the classical period. The IE suffix *-ti(i’ī»o/eh2)- 
appears in Armenian as -t`i, -di (with voicing of the *-t- after resonants) or -ti (under 
assimilatory influence of the preceding voiceless unaspirated stops; cf. lkti `lewd, 
licentious', apparently from lknim `to behave licentiously etc. (see 2.3.1, on *-ti-). 
Thus, ang-ti (originally *ank-ti, with secondary voicing like in ankanim/anganim) 
actually meant `the fallen one'. 
The synonymous samti (q.v.), also a hapax found next to angti, seems to be 
composed of the same suffix, but the root *sam- is otherwise unknown.

andruarandruarandruarandruar `cart, wagon; horse or mule yoked to a cart'. 
Attested in Agat`ange/lmidtildeos, ’ēüazar P`arpec`i, Severian of Gabala, John Chrysostom, 
etc. Spelled also as andr(u/a)var. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Mentioning earlier attempts to explain andruar as containing var- `to lead 
etc.', AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 192b) leaves the origin of the word open. A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 
20-22) connects with anur `ring', which is implausible. 
L. Hovhannisyan (1991a: 147) treats as composed of Iran. andar `interior' and var 
`cover' (seen also in ’å×an-uar `palanquin'), thus: `a cart with covered interior'. Being 
the best explanation known to me, this too is unconvincing. I propose an alternative 
etymology, though neither this is entirely convincing. 
Whether or not related (or contaminated) with var- `to lead etc.' or var- `to cover', 
the second component *war could be identic with that found in ’å×an-uar `palanquin' 
and eriw/var `fine horse'. As to *andr, one might assume that it has meant `cart, 
wagon' and is conneced with Skt. a’īüdhvan- m. `road' (RV+), OAv. aduuan-, YAv. 
a’üż’üó an- m. `road' from PIE *h1ndh-uen-; Skt. adhvara’īü- m. `(Soma-)sacrifice,

J’īīahukyan (1987: 112, 157, 592) reconstructs *anth- for Armenian and Greek and 
argues against AŌĆ░ar’īćyan's view, pointing out that the Georgian word has no 
Caucasian cognates, and adduces also Arm.

etymology conflicts with the dialectal variant *ant`r-. 
One wonders whether Hitt. ßĖ½anda’īäi’åÜ `warmth, heat' can be connected too (see s.v. 
xand `envy etc.'). 
We are possibly dealing with a Armeno-Greek(-Hittite?) word of substrate 
("Mediterranean") origin. For the suffixal element -/lmidtilde cf. other semantically close 
examples: Lat. cande’īäla `candle', Arm. xand-a/lmidtilde-, xanj-o

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM From PIE *h2eng-: Lat. angulus m. `corner, angle', OCS o’ī©gčŖlčŖ `corner', OIc. 
ekkja `ankle, heel', etc. The connection with Lat. angulus was already noted by 
Klaproth (1831=1823: 100a) and in NHB 1: 174c. See also Hu’īłbschmann 1897: 
419-420; HAB 1: 200b (also with forms that actually derive from *h2enk-, on which 
see s.v. an(u)t` `armpit'). 
According to Kortlandt (2003: 27), the absence of the development to *awc- 
"betrays a different ablautst ufe". As is pointed out by Beekes (2003: 204), however, 
this is irrelevant since ankiwn does not have a labiovelar. For the suffix see Olsen 
1999: 489-490, and 2.3.1. 
The Germanic, Slavic and Latin forms reflect full grade *h2eng-; for Lat. angulus, 
zero grade is possible, but unverifiable; Lat. ungulus `ring (on the finger)' and 
ungustus `crooked stick' derive from *h2ong- [Schrijver 1991: 43, 51, 60, 317]; see 
also Derksen 1996: 270-271. The absence of h- in Arm. ankiwn probably points to 
zero grade. This may be due to the derivation.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 202b) mentions the connection to anjuk `narrow' (q.v.) 
suggested implicitly in NHB 1: 190b. J’īīahukyan (1967: 163; with a question-mark - 
1987: 112; 1990: 10) and Olsen (1999: 355f, 784f) are more positive, though others 
(cf. Pokorny 1959: 42; Tumanjan 1978; Greppin 1983; etc.) do not mention anjaw 
next to anjuk. 
I see no serious semantic reason to reject the etymology, since anjuk very often 
refers to mountainous (narrow, cliffy, precipitous) places which are difficult to 
traverse. A similar development is seen in cognate forms too, such as Germ. Enge 
and Lat. angustum. For the semantic field `Angst; Bedra’īłngnis' : `stony/cliffy place' 
cf. vax `fear' vs. vax `precipitous/cliffy place'. 
The problem of -aw is more intriguing. Basing herself on Skt. am’īćhati’īü- f. 
`Bedra’īłngnis, Not' and OCS o*zota `Enge' and restoring an old " s/t-stem", Olsen 
(1999: 355-356, 784-785) derives anjaw < *anjawa- from *(h)ang^he/ota- through 
vowel assimilation a-e/o-a > a-a-a. However, the formation of Skt. am’īćhati’īü- is 
"ungewo’īłhnlich" [Mayrhofer, EWAia 1, 1992: 38], and I do n

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Corresponds to OIc. angi, n-stem m. `smell, scent', Dan. ange `Dampf', often 
derived from PIE *h2enh1- `to breathe' [Lide’īün 1906: 38-40; HAB 1: 203b; Pokorny 
1959: 43; Greppin 1983: 292; J’īīahukyan 1987: 112; Olsen 1999: 120]. It has been 
assumed that Osc. aftii’īüm `soul' belongs here, too (Knobloch 1974: 350; on this word 
see, however, Schrijver 1991: 30). 
If indeed from *h2enh1-g^h-, then anjn is another example of the loss of a laryngeal 
before a stop (*-RHC-; see 2.1.20).

Bible+. The evidence for the declension class comes from the substantive. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Lagarde (1854: 15 L352) and Hu’īłbschmann (1897: 420Nr34), derived from 
IE *h2(e)ng^h-u- `narrow': Skt. am’īćhu’īü-, MPers. *anzu’īäk, Goth. aggwu, etc.; cf. also PIE 
s-stem: Skt. a’īüm’īćhas- n. `Angst, Bedra’īłngnis', Lat. angus-tus; etc.; see HAB 1: 204; 
Pokorny 1959: 42-43; Tumanjan 1978: 63, 74, 125; 156; Schmitt 1981: 48, 50, 62, 
68; Greppin 1983: 292; Schrijver 1991: 43, 66; Mayrhofer, EWAia 1, 1992: 38-39; 
Olsen 1999: 588; etc. The reconstruction of a PIE labiovelar instead of the palatal 
(see Clackson 1994: 108 with lit.) seems unnecessary to me. On Armenian forms in 
-uk deriving from earlier *u-stems see Clackson 1994: 121-122. See also s.v. anjaw 
`cave'. 
The native origin of Arm. anjuk is accepted almost by everyone, except for 
Henning (followed by Mayr hofer, Salmons apud Mallory/Adams 1997: 391a), who 
treats it as an Iranian loan. This is possible, but unmotivated and unnecessary, since 
there is no reason to abandon the traditi onal point of view. In this respect some 
words on the suffix are in order.

Compare also Svedia *an-apur-d/t `uninhabited (place)' (q.v.).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM The component *-tar’īć has been frequently compared to IE *doru- `wood; tree' 
(see HAB s.v.v. targal, tarr, tor’īćn, torg, tram). Bugge (1890: 85-86) compares the 
correspondence car’īć `tree' vs. an-tar’īć `forest' (with an- from *sm’īź-; see also Sarad’å×eva 
1986: 36735) with cic : (merka)tit `teat', q.v. . AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 4: 671) connects antar’īć 
directly to Gr. ’üż’üź’Ć»’ü«’üż’ü▓’ü»’ü« , deriving the latter from *’üż’üź’Ć»’ü«’ü▓’ü»’ü« . J’īīahukyan (1987: 118, 245, 
258, 259; 1988, 2: 80) restores *sm’īź-d╔Öru- with a question-mark. Earlier, he (1967: 
182, 303; cf. also NHB 1: 243a) equated the *tar’īć with Arm. car’īć `tree' (q.v.), placing 
antar’īć in the list of words with alternation c/t. Neither of these etymologies explains 
the phonological development of the word satisfactorily. 
The reconstruction of *sm’īź-dVru- would be possible if we assume a contamination 
with car’īć `tree'. It becomes easier when the *tar’īć is directly connected to tarr/tar’īć 
`elementum' (q.v.) (which probably originates from the same *doru- `wood; tree'), 
though the latter alternant, that is tar’īć, is postclassical. The semantic relationship

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Composed as ar’īć- + ag- `to put on (clothes)' (see also s.v. awt`oc`) + -ast 
[NHB 1: 281c; HAB 1: 248b; J’īīahukyan 1987: 123]. Meillet (1936: 77) and J’īīahukyan 
(1987: 240) derive the ending from a compound suffix *-s-ti-, whereas Weitenberg 
(1980: 213, 214) assumes a suffix -st- which has resulted from generalization of 
*-u-k-ti-. 
One wonders if ar’īć-agast is related with z-gest, u-stem `clothing'. The absence of 
the initial laryngeal in *ues- (cf. Hitt. u’īü-e-e’åÜ-ta `wears', Gr. ’üź’éō’ü«’ü«’üĄ’üŁ’ü® , -’üŁ’üĪ’ü® `I clothe') 
seems to obstackle the equation, unless one accepts the explanation given by 
Kortlandt (2003: 43). Contamination is possible, too. It is interesting that the 
i-stem 
of ar’īćagast agrees with what might be expected for zgest (cf. Lat. uestis `cloth, 
garment'; Goth. wasti `garment, dress'), though in reality zgest has u-stem. On the 
other hand, the parallel a-stem of ar’īćagast is reminiscent of formations like Gr. 
(Hesychius) ’ü¦’üź’ü│’ü┤’ü®’Ć»’üĪ `clothing' < *ues-tih2- or ’üź’Ć¦’ü│’ü▒’ü©’Ć»’ü¬ `clothing' < *ues-th2-(?) (cf. 
also ’üź’éö’ü│’ü▒’ü»’ü¬ n.). One may therefore propose an alternative solution: NSg *ue’īüs-t

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM The word is composed of the prefix ar’īć- (rather than ar’īća- as suggested in 
Olsen 1999: 72), the anaptyxis -a- before s (cf. Greppin 1983: 297; J’īīahukyan 1987: 
243; see s.v.v. ar’īćasta/lmidtilde and ar’īć- ), and otherwise unattested root *spel-, which is 
derived from PIE *spel-. (In Hovhannisyan 1990: 65, a- is a "prothetic vowel", 
which is not quite accurat). 
This etymology has been proposed by Lide’īün (1933: 46-49) and is accepted 
generally (HAB 1: 253-254; Pokorny 1959: 985; Solta 1960: 288; Klingenschmitt 
1982: 169f; Mallory/Adams 1997: 536; Olsen 1999: 72; etc.). Compare Goth. spill 
`story, fable', Alb. fja’īüle’īł f. (Sg, Pl) `word' (Demiraj 1997: 134, in passing), Gr. 
’üĪ’Ć¦’ü░’üź’ü®’ü¼’ü©’Ć» `threat; promise', ’üĪ’Ć¦’ü░’üź’ü®’ü¼’üź’Ć»’üĘ `to threaten', cf. Beekes 1969: 50, 85; 
Mallory/Adams 1997: 536 ("if from *n’īź-peln’Ä┐"). The appurtenance of Toch. B p’ćżl- 
`to praise, commend' is uncertain [Adams 1999: 376-377]. According to AŌĆ░ar’īćyan 
(HAB 1: 253-254), Tumanyan (1978: 204) et al., only the Germanic words are 
related to the Armenian. Greppin (1981b: 3) notes that the correlation Arm. ar’īćaspel

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Another case of the composition of the prefix ar’īć(a)- and an independently 
unattested root (cf. s.v.v. ar’īć- and ar’īćaspel), that is *sta/lmidtilde. The latter is connected 
(Dervischjan 1877: 40 1 and Lide’īün 1933: 41-42, 45, independently) to OCS stelja 
`roof' and the like, which can be seen under Pokorny's *stel-2 `ausbreiten, flach 
hinbreiten' (see from. Everyone accepts this (Pokorny 1959: 1018-1019; Solta 1960: 
225ff; Tumanjan 1978: 204-205; Greppin 1983: 297-298; J’īīahukyan 1987: 151; 
Olsen 1999: 208; etc.) without mentioning the alternative etymology proposed by 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 254), who prefers deriving Arm. *sta/lmidtilde from what is presented in 
Pokorny 1959: 1019-1020 as *stel-3. 
Both AŌĆ░ar’īćyan and Pokorny ("wohl") point out the possibility that these two PIE 
roots may be related to each other. Howeve r, we will continue dealing with the 
"Wurzel-etymologie" untill we recognize the direct association of Armenian *sta/lmidtilde 
with Gr. ’ü│’ü┤’ü©’Ć»’ü¼’ü© `block or slab used as a memorial; monument; gravestone; post, 
pillar; boundary-post' and OHG stollo, MHG stolle `St’ć╝tze,

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 256) does not accept any of numerous etymological 
proposals, of which only that of Patruba’īüny (*1906: 341//HAms) is worth of 
consideration. He analyzes the word as ar’īć- + *aw- + -awt and compares the root 
*aw- with Lat. aur’Ä┐ra f. `dawn', Gr. ’üź’éō’üĘ’ü¬ , ’üĪ’üĄ’éö’üĘ’ü¬ f. `dawn', Skt. us’īŻa’īüs- f. `morning 
light, morning, dawn' (RV+), etc. This etymology is advocated by Dume’īüzil (see 
Greppin 1983: 298 for references), and, with some reservation, by J’īīahukyan (1987: 
114, 159, 383); cf. also Eichner 1978: 152 34; Clackson 1994: 223 97, 224 98; Olsen 
1999: 95944. See also s.v. ayg `morning'. 
A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 24-27) derives *aw- from the root of PIE *sa’īäu-el- `sun'. This is 
improbable since, as stated by J’īīahukyan (1987: 159) the "pure" root *sa’īäu- is not 
attested in any cognate language. A/lmidtildeayan (ibid.) identifies the -aw-awt with the hapax 
awo’īät (meaning `time' according to AŌĆ░ar’īćyan [HAB 1: 363a], and `the time of 
sun-rise' according to E’īä. A/lmidtildeayan), also found in ’åÜa/lmidtilde-awo’īät (with ’åÜa/lmidtilde `dew' as the first 
member) and kam-awo’īät attested

ar’īću `brook, channel, ditch'. 
The initial a- is prothetic, though this (together with ar’īću) is the only unambiguous 
case of a prothetic vowel before the trilled r’īć; ar’īćewc `lion' (q.v.) is probably of 
onomatopoeic origin. The absence of a prothetic vowel in r’īćungn `nose, nostrils' 
suggests a loan or substrate orig in. It has been suggested that ar’īću derives from *er’īću 
(see Greppin 1983: 301), and the o- of or’īćogem is due to assimilatory influence of the 
root vowel, see Klingenschmitt 1982: 20452; Beekes 2003: 160-161 (from *e-r’īćogem).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM The only etymology I know of is that of A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 29Nr13) who derives it 
from PIE *h2eHs- `hearth; ash'. For the cognates see s.v. azazim and aŌĆ░iwn. With the 
exception of Greppin (1983: 303), this etymology is unknown to the western 
scholars. Even in Armenia proper it remained unnoticed, except for A/lmidtildeabekyan 1979:

entry between square brackets. 
Though not very clear, the etymology is, nevertheless, worthy of consideration. 
For the semantics cf. 
kayc `spark' vs. `ruby', Gr. ’üĪ’éö’ü«’ü▒’ü▓’üĪ’üĖ `charcoal' vs. `ruby,

and the possible influence of aŌĆ░iwn `ash' (if this is indeed related), where the initial 
syllable of the historically polysyllabic form was unstressed. The suffixal element -k- 
can go back to PIE *-g- which is probably attested in OIc. aska `ash', Gr.

jkambk`: naew k`a/lmidtildeak`in ayn isk hanapazord asŌĆ░i. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymology. 
I wonder whether it can go back to IE `ash'; cf. OIc. aska `ash'; Arm. askn prob. 
`ruby' < *`ember, spark' (q.v.), aŌĆ░iwn < *a’åÜŌĆ░iwn `ash' (q.v.). If this is true, asŌĆ░i may 
be derived from *h2Hs-g-ii’ī»V- `of ash' and referred originally to food (bread?) 
prepared (baked?) by burying it under ashes. The underlying practice is parallel to 
that reflected in Arm. nkan `a kind of bread' < Iran. *ni-kan- `buried', cf. Khwar. 
kand `bread', etc.; see HAB 3: 455-456. 
If the loss of -s- in aŌĆ░iwn `ash' is conditioned by pretonic position, we have a 
discrepancy in asŌĆ░i. Therefore it would perhaps be better to treat asŌĆ░i as a late 
derivation from *ask `ash' with the productive suffix -i, or else as a (derivation based 
on the) petrified Locative; cf. s v. ayg `dawn'. 
However, this word will remain uncertain until further textual support is found.

*h2rh3trom (the zero grade of this type being showed by Lith. i’īĆrklas `oar' from 
*h1rh1-), and the variant harawr, as Lith. a’īürklas and Lat. ar’É░trum, adopted the e-grade 
of the verb. This is accepted by Beekes (2003 1183, 193). [Earlier, Beekes (1969: 
140, 231) assumed a full grade. According to Lindeman (1982: 40-41), Lat. ara’īäre 
and PArm. *ara’īä- (unpreserved) may reflect an iterative in *-a’īä- with zero grade in the 
root syllable: * h2rH-eh2-ye-]. Olsen (1999: 35, 765-769, 846) disagrees with this 
view and restores a full grade of the root. One wonders whether we can dismiss 
Celtic (from *h2erh3-tro- in Schrijver 1991: 108) and Germanic forms as evidence for 
the full grade. 
At any rate, Kortlandt's explanation is preferable since it show

non-IE origin [Beekes apud Mallory/Adams 1997: 499b]. J’īīahukyan (1987: 601) 
points out the correspondence between IE and WCauc. forms (Abkhaz saba `dust' 
etc.). For the problem of the initial h- in ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde as a reflex of IE *s- see AŌĆ░ar’īćyan, 
HLP 2, 1951: 411 (with a question-mark); N. Simonyan 1979: 211, 213 (sceptical). 
 
8 I wonder if Me/lmidtilderi ha’īłrg aril `to finish, make an end' (recorded in A/lmidtildeayan 1954: 312, in the 
glossary of dialectal words) reflects *y-arg `(at/in) end'.

The form, however, is usually derived from *Hou-tleh2- (cf. Lat. sub’ģ½cula `woolen

According to Olsen (1999: 531), the second component in

Lat. a’īätriensis `house servant' from a’īätrium. The composition would correspond, as 
she points out, to Avest. a’īätr╔Ö-k╔Ör╔Öt- `der sich mit dem Feuer zu tun macht, dabei 
ta’īłtig ist'. 
As far as the second component is con cerned, ’ēüap`anc`yan's etymology seems 
semantically more probable. As for the fi rst component *awri-, one may suggest an 
old borrowing from Iran. *ahur-i- `lordly' (cf. YAv. ’É░h’ģ½iri- adj. `with regard to

numerous times in e.g. HZ’īīHek` 6, 1973: 633-636. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 389b) does not accept any of the etymological suggestions 
and leaves the origin open. Schulth ei’ć¤ (1961: 221) compares with Hitt. u’ī»alßĖ½- 
`schlagen'. J’īīahukyan (1987: 317) rightly rejects the comparison since the initial u’ī»-

Strangely enough, the obvious onomatopoeic origin of bax- (suggested in NHB 1: 
423c) is largely ignored. Onomatopoeic are perhaps also Laz and Mingr. bax(-) `to 
beat', though AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 389b) treats them as Armenian borrowings. 
’ēüap`anc`yan (1975: 353) considers this view to be unverifiable and points out the 
onomatopoeic character of the word. Note also Russ. bac, babax(-), Engl. bang, etc.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 402b). 
V. Ar’īćak`elyan (1979: 37; 1981: 77) assumes that bay-oc` is identic with dial.

Moreover, bay-oc` `den, lair' is directly confirmed by the dialect of Xotorj’īīur (see 
above). 
A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 35-36) connects the word with OIr. both `hut', Welsh bod 
`dwelling'; Lith. bu’īĆtas `house', etc. from *bh(e)uH- `to be'; see s.v.v. boyn `nest; 
den, lair; hut', boys `plant', etc. According to J’īīahukyan (1987: 116, 160), the IE 
proto-form may have been *bhu’ī»a’īä-t- (= *bhu’ī»eH-t-), and the closest cognate - Alb. 
bo’īüt/e’īł, -a f. `Lehmsorte (zum Polieren); Boden; Erde; Welt' (< *bhu’ī»e’īä/a’īä-ta’īä-). On the 
latter see Demiraj 1997: 107. 
Not all the formal details are clear. For the semantic field cf. etymologically 
cognate Arm. boyn `nest; den, lair; hut', Skt. bhu’īüvana- n. `Wesen; Welt' (RV+), etc.

bilbilbilbil `light-blue' (?). 
Attested only in Step`annos Siwnec`i (8th cent.), denoting a kind of fish. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM NHB (1: 489b; cf. 2: 652c) takes the word to mean `light-blue' and compares 
it with Arm. dial. pluz `blue', Ital. blu’īĆ, etc. Abe/lmidtildeyan and AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (see HAB 1: 450) 
refuse the meaning `light-blue'. After an extensive discussion, however, A/lmidtildeayan 
(1974: 44-47) advocates the basic meaning `light-blue', which has developped into 
the fish-name (cf. the fish-name kapoyt which follows bil in the list). Then he 
connects bil with OCS be’īīlčŖ, Russ. be’īülyj `white', etc. from PIE *bh’ÉĄlH-, see also s.v. 
bal `mist, fog; (dial). white fleck'. The same etymology has been proposed 
independently by Sarad’å×eva (1976: 191; 1980c; 1986: 97-98). The etymology is 
accepted by J’īīahukyan (1987: 115, 160, 270). For the semantics cf. lurt`/j’īī `light, 
shiny; light-blue'. Sarad’å×eva (1986: 375 18) wonders if Arm. pluz `blue' (Ararat pliz, 
Agulis pl/cturnz, see HAB 4: 87b) is related with Engl. blue etc.; cf. the idea of NHB 
above. 
Compare also *b/lmidtilde-e’īät (see HAB 1: 456a).

source: Martirosyan, H. (2011), Studies in Armenian Etymology
title: remarkably
snippet:
kind of cake' [HAB 1: 454]. The 
meaning in Moks ( p╔Ölit`, GSg p╔Ölt`╔ÖŽĄ) is thouroughly described in Orbeli 2002: 312. 
Remarkably, Ararat, Moks etc. have also the meaning `a small swelling' [Amatuni 
1912: 105a]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 1: 454a. J’īīahukyan (1987: 117, 
161) derives from PIE *bhl-ei- `to swell' (cf. Gr. ’ü”’ü¼’ü® ’üż’üĪ’Ć»’üĘ etc.). This proto-form 
would yield Arm. *e-/lmidtildebi-, however. Olsen (1999: 244, 948) places in the list of 
words of unknown origin, not mentioning any etymology. 
The semantics of blit` (`a roundish soft bread; lobe of the ear or the liver; (dial.) 
swelling') is remarkably close to that of boy-t` `lobe of the ear or the liver; thumb; 
hump'; `young of a frog' (q.v.). The basic meaning is `a soft lump of something; 
swelling; a roundish projecting part of the body' < `swollen, grown'. One may 
therefore derive bl-it` from *bul < PIE *bhuH-l-, from the root *bheuH- 'to grow'. 
The full grade is reflected in boyl (q.v.). Note that both bl-it` an boyt` (if from 
*bu-it`) contain the suffix -it` (see 2.3.1). Since boyl has an i-stem comparable with 
IIran. *bhu’īär-i- `abundant'), one wonders whether the vocalism of the suffix in bl-it` 
can be explained by the same *-i-; thus: *bul-i’īü-thV- > blit`.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM According to AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 4: 95a; cf. also 2: 229a and AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 
201a), the root is *bol- which is a Caucasian loan; cf. reduplicated forms Georg. 
boboli `a large worm', Laz boboli `insect'. 
This solution is too narrow and unsatisfactory. First of all, *bo/u- `spider, 
tarantula; scorpion; ghost', ranging from Polis and Nor Naxij’īīewan to ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, are’åÜ 
etc., which AŌĆ░ar’īćyan mentions only as a semantic parallel, seems to be related too. 
Note also reduplicated *bo-bo which is not necessarily a reduced form of *bo-bol//lmidtilde. 
Secondly, the spread of this word in neighbouring languages, as we shall see, is 
much wider. 
Klimov (1998: 145) represents Kartvel. *oboba- `spider': Georg. oboba- `spider', 
Megrel. bo(r)bolia- < *bo(r)bo-, with dimin. -ia, Laz bobon’łŹva- < *bobo-, Svan 
*opopa, wopopa’īł, etc.

is compared with Arab. bu’īä, abu’īä `tarantula' [S. Vardanyan 1990: 613, note 616/2].

This word is not attested independently. I tentatively restore it on the basis of 
some dialectal evidence (see below) and its hypothetical connection with bor 
`leprosy' and boreni `hyena' (q.v.). 
’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Karin borek is described by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (1913: 203b) as " t`ux, ŌĆ░ermak goynov

(spitak goynov ). It is not quite clear what he means exactly; perhaps `a 
dark-complexioned cow with white spots'.

Lately attested. Much older and widespread is bor-ot `leprous' (Bible+) > `bad; 
unpure, dirty; heretic' (for the semantic field see 3.5.2.2). 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Considered to be a loan from Iran. *bor `leprosy', only preserved in Sogd. 
’üó r'wk' /’üó aru’īäk╔Ö/ `leprous' [HAB 1: 474b; J’īīahukyan 1987: 520]. Bearing in mind the 
Iranian alternation b- : v- (cf. e.g. the word for `violet' - see 2.3.1 on -aw’åÜ, see also 
s.v. mrj’īīiwn `ant'), one may assume that Arm. uruk `leprous', which, to my 
knowledge, has not received an etymological explanation, is borrowed from Iran. 
*voru’īäk- through an intermediary *wuruk. 
It seems that the forms are related with *bor `brown or motley/spotted' (q.v.). For 
the semantics cf. Arm. pisak `spotted; leprous', dial. of Van and ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde p`is `dirty' : 
Pers. p’ē¦s `leprous; dirty' (see HAB 4: 84b; AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1902: 352); cf. also Gr. ’üĪ’Ć¦’ü¼’ü”’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ 
m. `dull-white leprosy' (Hes.) from `white' (cf. Lat. albus `white, pale, bright, clear', 
etc.). The above-mentioned Sogdian form may be derived from *bher-u- (or *bhe-bhr-
u-?). For more detail see s.v.v. *bor `brown animal', boreni `hyena'.

title: borean

’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Ararat bor-ani `coat of a fur of hyena' [AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 203a; HAB 1: 477b; 
HayLezBrbBar’īć 1, 2001: 211b]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 477) and, independently, J’īīahukyan (1965: 253; see also 
1987: 116, 160) derive the word from the o-grade of PIE *bher- `brown' (also 
characterizing animals), cf. Lith. be’īć’īüras `brown', OHG bero `bear', etc. The only 
cognate in o-grade cited by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan and J’īīahukyan is Slav. *bobr- `beaver', but this 
in fact is a reduplicated form. J’īīahukyan (1972: 284; 1987: 116) adds here also dial. 
(Karin) borekborekborekborek `grey, white cow' (see s.v. *bor `brown animal, etc.'). 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 160; cf. Olsen 1999: 414) alternatively suggests the Iranian 
origin of boreni, cf. YAv. ba’üó ra- m. `beaver'. As is pointed out by J’īīahukyan, the 
Iranian is semantically remote. However, this is not a serious problem since the other 
meanings may have been lost in Iranian. It will be remembered that Skt. babhru- 
refers to other animals too, cf. `a kind of ichneumon', `a reddish-brown cow' 
(compare the meaning of Arm. dial. borek `a dark-complexioned cow'), etc. For 
other possibly related Armenian forms see s.v. *bor.

These suggestions seem unnecessary since brdor’īć is transparently composed of

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 532-533) derives from PIE *uel- `to see', cf. Lat. voltus, 
vultus, -’ģ½s m. `countenance, facial expression; face; looks, features', Bret. guelet `la 
vue', etc. See also Olsen 1999: 51. 
As we have seen, Thomson (1978: 141 17) considers Movse’īäs' etymology of 
Ange/lmidtildeeay as "fanciful". However, mythical creatures and giants are often 
characterized as `unshaped, deformed' or the like, containing the privative prefix an-, 
cf. e.g. s.v. ard. The basic meaning of *ge/lmidtilde is `appearance, shape; seeing' (cf. PIE `to 
see'), and the interpretation of Ange/lmidtildeeay as `shapeless, deformed' or `not having an 
appearance', whether etymological or folk-e tymological, is not necessarily a product 
of Movse’īäs' fantasy. 
The formation of *an-ge/lmidtilde may also be understood as `the Un-seen' (or 
`not-seeng'?); cf. Gr. '’üü’ü® ’Ć»’üż’ü©’ü¬ , etc. For further discussion see s.v. Ange/lmidtildeeay.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM There cam be no doubt that getar’īć derives from get `river' (q.v.). AŌĆ░ar’īćyan 
(HAB 1: 537) does not specify the component -ar’īć. All the meanings can theoretically 
presuppose a basic semantics `to flow, stream'. A river-bed is the bed or channel in 
which a river flows ; a river-shore is the land that is watered by the river; an 
outbranching "mother-river" is a river that makes flow a rivulet from itself. The 
component -ar’īć can be derived from PIE *sr(o)u- `to stream, flow', cf. Skt. srav- `to 
stream, flow', Russ. struja’īü `stream', Lith. srauja’īĆ, Latv. strauja `stream', etc. In this 
case, it is identic with Arm. ar’īću `brook, tributary; channel; ditch, trench, furrow, 
passage' (q.v.). The fact that in the oldest attestation we find getar’īću, with final u-, 
makes the connection even more transparent. The semantic development `to stream, 
flow' > `irrigated, watered land' is also seen in Russ. ostrov `island' from the same 
PIE *sr(o)u-. 
The ORuss. river-name ąöčŖąĮčŻčüčéčĆčŖ (cf. ’üä’üĪ’Ć»’ü«’üĪ’ü│’ü┤’ü▓’ü®’ü¬ etc.) has been interpreted as of 
Iranian origin, containing the word for `river', cf. Avest. da’īän

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 1: 541) derives from PIE *gherdh-: Skt. gr’īźha’īü- m. `house, 
residence' (RV+), YAv. g╔Ör╔Ö’üż a- m. `house of da’ÉĄvic beings', Goth. gards m. `house, 
housekeeping', etc. As he po ints out, the absence of palatalization of the initial 
guttural is problematic (on this see 2.1.14), and -stan (of Iranian origin) is also found 
with native roots, cf. and `cornfield' : and-astan, etc. 
It has been assumed that Arm. gerd-astan derives from the same PIE word but via 
Iranian intermediation [Brandenstein/Mayrhofer 1964: 120; Nyberg 1974: 80; 
J’īīahukyan 1987: 171, 272, 520; Olsen 1999: 333, 333 290]. For the semantic 
development `house, household, estate' > `servant' cf. especially OPers. *garda- 
`Diener, Hausgesinde, ’ü»’ü®’Ć¦’ü½’üź’Ć»’ü┤’ü©’ü¬ ', Pahl. ga’īäl [g'l] coll. `the gang, the villeins labouring 
on the estates of the kings, the satraps, the magnates, etc.'; see s.v. a/lmidtildeaxin `female

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM From PIE *u(e)id-: Skt. aor. a’īüvidat (= e-git `he found'), pres. vinda’īüti `to find' 
(RV+), Pahl. wind- `to find; to desire', Lat. uid’ÉĄre `to look, to see', etc. 
[Hu’īłbschmann 1897: 437; HAB 1: 564; Schmitt 1981: 49, 54]. 
According to Meillet (1936: 44), giwt (i-stem) derives from *uind-. For this and 
"epenthetical" explanations I refer to Clackson 1994: 108, 221 55 and, especially, 155. 
Olsen (1999: 182-183) relates the u-epenthesis to *uid-tu- continued in Lat. vi’īäsus 
`look'. Beekes (2003: 205) points out that giwt "clearly belongs to the root git-, and 
it is quite possible that the epenthesis was caused by a following u, but it cannot be 
demonstrated". 
Winter (1962: 261) explains giwt from PIE *uid-ti-, with a development of *-dt- 
to -wt-. Clackson (1994: 155) considers this explanation as the most preferable. See 
2.1.22.12 more detail. In this case, Arm. an-giwt adj. `not found' (Koriwn, P`awstos, 
’ēüazar P`arpec`i, E/lmidtildei’åÜe’īä) would match Skt. a’īü-vitti- f. `not-finding' (AV). 
It is tempting to compare Arm. dial. *gntn- with Skt. vinda’īüti `to find' (RV+),

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Usually derived from IE *ghom-, only with Germanic (gemination 
presumably from *-mn-): Dan. gamme `sheepfold', Swed. dial. gamme `crib, 
manger', OIc. gammi m. `Lappenh’ć╝tte, Erdh’ć╝tte', Swiss g’ćżmmeli `Viehh’ć╝tte', etc. 
[Lide’īün 1906: 14-16; HAB 1: 574-575; Pokorny 1959: 452; J’īīahukyan 1987: 128]. 
The etymology has been doubted since the expected reflex is *gun (J’īīahukyan 
1987: 171, cf. 254) or *gum (Olsen 1999: 198). Olsen (ibid.) reconsructs 
*ghos-mo-/-eh2- connecting with Skt. ghas- `to eat' etc., assuming an original 
meaning `eating place'. [For the phonetic development see s.v. hoyn/hon 
`cornel-tree']. 
One may assume that the vocalic development has been blocked by geminatton 
(*-mn- > *-mm-?), as in *pen-nu-mi > henum (see s.v. *hin- `to weave'), or by the 
lowering influence of the a in the following syllable: *ghom-eh2- > PArm. *goma-, 
cf. don `a kind of bread', if from PArm. *dona- < PIE *dhoH-neh2- `grain; bread' 
(see s.v.). Of borrowings, note com `fasting, abstinence from food' < Syriac s’īŻo’īäm or 
s’īŻo’īäma’īä.

interesting. Is there an ETYM ology? Cf. also Arm. dial. durgar `carpenter' 
[HayLezBrbBar’īć 1, 2001: 362a], which seems to reflect an Iranian compound with 
*ka’īär- `to work' as the second member. 
J’īīahukyan (1967: 152) derives dur from IE *der- (cf. Skt. da’īära- etc.; see s.v. 
ter’īćem), placing it in the list of words which did not participate in the consonant shift. 
This is uncertain. Later he (J’īīahukyan 1987: 265) mentions dur among non-native 
designations of the semantic field of craft without any further specification. 
I suggest a tentative comparison to IIr. *dha’īära’īä- f. `Klinge, Schneide': Skt. dha’īä’īüra’īä- 
f. `Schneide (des Schwertes, des Messers), Klinge' (RV+), YAv. f. da’īära’īä- `Klinge', 
Khot. da’īära’īä- `Schneide', etc.; see Mayrhofer, EWAia 1, 1992: 789; E’īĆtimSlovIranJaz 
2, 2003: 451. According to Mayrhofer, the IIr. word "ist wohl als `*Gu’ć¤' (> 
`Klinge', beim Erkalten des Kupfers) mit dha’īä’īüra’īä-1 [f. `Strom, Strahl, Gu’ć¤'] identisch". 
The latter is "vielleicht" from *dhn’īź’īüHreh2-. This etymology, however, does not seem 
very probable. One might connect IIr. *dha’īära’īä- f. `Klinge, Schneide' to Arm. dur 
`

Beekes (apud Mallory/Adams 1997: 343b) considers the connection between BSl. 
*h1eg^h-er- (not mentioning Arm. ezr) with Lith. e’å×ia’īĆ etc. to be uncertain. There seem 
to be no solid reasons for this. Meanings such as `mill-pond', `drain, canal' and 
`brook' form a semantic link between *je’īī’å×-/je’å×- `dam, wier' and *jezero `lake'. 
Besides, the Armenian word is an intermediary form since it is semantically identic 
with Lith. 
e’å×ia’īĆ but formally closer to Lith. e~’å×eras `lake', OCS jezero n. `lake', etc.

’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Ham’åÜen e/lmidtildeim `icicle', ’ēüazax e/lmidtildem-a-kal-el `to be covered by hoarfroast' [HAB 
2: 17a]. Also Dersim yŽĄ/lmidtildeyam [Ba/lmidtilderamyan 1960: 78b]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 2: 16-17. A/lmidtildeayan (1980: 142) 
analyzes as *e/lmidtildei-amn for the formation comparing with ayceamn `gazelle, roe' < 
*ayci- + -amn (see s.v. ayc(i) `goat' and 2.3.1). Olsen (1999: 376, 943) mentions as a 
word of unknown origin containing the suffix -eamn. 
I propose to comare Arm. *e/lmidtildei- with Balto-Slav. *h1iH-ni- `hoar-frost, rime' (cf. 
Beekes apud Mallory/Adams 1997: 287a): Russ. i’īünej, Czech ji’īüni’īü, SCr. i’īänje, Bulg. 
i’īünej, Lith. y’īünis (dial.), etc. The full grade of the word, viz. *h1eiH-ni-, may have 
yielded PArm. *ei╔Öni- > *e(i)eni- > *eni-, with assimilation (see 2.1.23) and 
subsequent loss of *-╔Ö-. Alternatively, one may assume a zero grade root: *h1iH-ni- 
> PArm. *ini-a’īümVn > *(i)/lmidtildeiamn (with dissimilation n ... n > /lmidtilde ... n, and loss of 
word-initial pretonic i-, see 2.1.33.2) > e-/lmidtildeeamn, with a regular prothetic e- before /lmidtilde. 
For the suffix cf. sar’īćamanik` `ice'. Thus: *eni-am(a)n > e/lmidtildeeamn with nasal 
dissimilation.

Frequent in the Bible, referring to the breast of sacrificial animals and rendering 
Gr. ’ü│’ü┤’ü©’ü▒’üĄ’Ć»’ü«’ü®’ü»’ü« (dimin.) `breast'. For apposition with ’üó’ü▓’üĪ’üŻ’ü®’Ć»’üĘ’ü« = eri `shoulder of 
animals' see there. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymology is accepted in HAB 2: 42b. 
Lide’īün (1937: 92) derives from IE *bhrug^o- or *bhrugo- with Gr. ’ü”’üĪ’Ć»’ü▓’üĄ’ü¦’üĖ , gen. 
-’üĄ’ü¦’ü»’ü¬ , - ’üĄ’ü¦’ü¦’ü»’ü¬ `throat; dewlap of a bull', and Lat. fr’ģ½men `throat' < *fr’ģ½’īåg-smen. He 
is sceptical about Goth. brusts `breast', Russ. brju’īüxo `belly', etc. The etymology is 
accepted in J’īīahukyan 1987: 116, 262; Olsen 1999: 49. The metathesis *bhr- > Arm. 
erb- is regular, see par. XX. 
Olsen (ibid.) derives erbuc from nom. *bhrug/g^-s assuming that *g^ and *g^s would 
coalesce in Arm. c. If the -c` in ere’īäc` `elder' (q.v.) reflects *sgw- (cf. Gr. ’ü░’ü▓’üź’Ć»’ü│’üó’üĄ’ü¬ ), 
the -c of erbuc should rather be explained by non-nominative forms. In view of the 
absence of other examples, however, this must be viewed as yet uncertain.

title: mountainous
snippet:
240 
’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Dialectally ubiquitous. Kusget (Motkan) i’åÜvil means `to go', since the area ist 
mountainous, and going is equivalent to going down [HAB 2: 119b; 4: 655b]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM From PIE *h1ei-gh- or *h1ei-dh- `to go': Gr. ’ü»’ü®’éö’üŻ’ü»’üŁ’üĪ’ü® `to go'; Lith. eiga’īĆ 
'Gang, Verlauf'; OCS iti `to go', isg ido’ī©; cf. Skt. e’īüti `to go'; Gk. ’üź’ü® ’éÆ’üŁ’ü® `to go'; Lith. 
ei~ti `to go'; etc.; see Pedersen 1982: 203 (=1906: 425; 1907: 146); Scheftelowitz 
(**BB 28: 311); HAB 2: 119a, 4: 655b; Klingenschmitt 1982: 207-208; J’īīahukyan 
(D’å×aukjan) 1982: 59; 1987: 121, 436. See also s.v. *eham `to go'. {{NOTE - Olsen 
(1999: 17 29) alternatively suggests a connection with Skt. si’īüdhyati `to succeed, be 
successful', which is semantically remote, however. - ENDNOTE}}. 
Armenian demonstrates a semantic shift `to go' > `to go down', cf. the 
above-mentioned dialectal (Kusget) meaning. If it has not preserved the original 
meaning, this dialect represents the result of a twofold semantic shift: PIE `to go' > 
Arm. `to go down' > `to go'.

title: two
snippet:
Ararat, ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde). The latter 
two, as well as other foods made of milkproducts are represented by various 
compounds that often contain t`an, see AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 347b-349b; HayLezBrbBar’īć 2, 
2002: 76b-79b. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 149b) treats t`an as derived from t`anam, 1SgAor. t`ac`i `to 
make wet' (q.v.), as has already been suggested in NHB 1: 794c. Accepted by 
J’īīahukyan 1987: 152; Olsen 1999: 197. 
The etymology may be correct. However, the semantic development should be 
examined more closely. To establish the basic meaning of t`an as `wet, liquid food', 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan introduces an interesting parallel - dial. *t`ac`an *from t`ac` `wet', that is a 
generic term for milk products, but also denotes `anything to eat with bread'. This 
seems to be a secondary de rivation referring to any additional food which in 
combination with bread would make a simple meal. Compare also *hac`(-u)-t`ac` 
`food' [AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 648b]. The semantic motivation here is, thus, not exactly the 
same as in the case of t`an, which in literature refers to pottage, and in dialects - to 
milk products and food made of milk products. Therefore, not denying the 
probability of the traditional etymology, I propose an alternative, which would 
involve a more reasonable (at least in my view) semantic development and, more

title: maraa
snippet:
0L8 = 1, 1983: 340). Formally, this represents the pure root *t`ar*t`ar*t`ar*t`ar’īć’īć’īć’īć----, 
though one cannot be sure that it is not a recent analogical formation. Note that 
formulae can have preserved archaisms. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since long, connected with Skt. tars’īŻ-: tr’īź’īüs’īŻyant- `to be thirsty, to crave', YAv. 
tar’åÜu- `dry, not fluid', Gr. ’ü┤’üź’Ć»’ü▓’ü│’ü»’üŁ’üĪ’ü® `to become dry', Hitt. tar’åÜ- `to dry', etc. (see 
HAB 2: 155-156). 
Pedersen (1906: 413 = 1982: 191) explains Arm. -r’åÜ- from *-rsi’ī»- (: Skt.tr’īź’īüs’īŻyati), 
comparing gar’åÜ- : Skt. hr’īźs’īŻyati (see s.v.). This is accepted by Meillet (1950: 85). See, 
however, 2.1.12. 
The twofold reflex of PIE *rs in t`ar’åÜamim : t`ar’īćamim `to wither' is considered to 
be one of the oldest traces of early dialectal diversity. In order to evaluate this reflex, 
one should try establishing the pholological background of the distribution. 
The adjective t`ar’īćam and ant`ar’īćam, as well as the verb t`ar’åÜamim are reliably 
attested since the 5th century, whereas the adjective an-t`ar’åÜam is found only once in 
the old period, *t`ar’åÜam is not attested at all, and the verbal t`ar’īćam- is found only in 
participle and causative, each of them - once in the Bible. That the verb t`ar’åÜamim is 
old and archaic may be indirectly confirmed by its disappearance from modern 
dialects and replacement by t`

’ü┤’üĪ’ü▓’ü░’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ , ’ü┤’üź’ü▓’ü░’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ m., ’ü┤’üĪ’ü▓’ü░’ü»’Ć»’ü«’ü© f. `id.'. The Greek and Armenian are usually derived 
from PIE *tu’ī»(e)r-p- : *tu’ī»erH- `to grab, enclose', cf. Lith. tve’īürti `to seize, form', OCS 
tvoriti `to do, make'; see Pokorny 1959: 1101 (without Armenian); J’īīahukyan 1987: 
154, 302. According to Clackson (1994: 183), we are probably dealing with a 
common borrowing from a lost source. 
The QIE cluster *-rp- regularly yields Arm. -rb-. In this case, the by-form t`arp` 
presents us with the problem of -p`. One might assume a non-IE *tarph-, with 
aspirated *-ph-, or assimilation t`...b > t`...p`, especially after r (on the latter 
circumstance see above). However, the by-form with -b seems to be reliable. I 
therefore propose an alternative solution which can explain the allophones p` : b. 
Gr. ’ü┤’üĪ’Ć»’ü▓’ü░’ü© derives from QIE *t(a)rp-eh2-. If we may posit a HD laryngeal-stem, 
the paradigm would have been as follows: nom. *to’īürp-eh2- (or *terp-eh2-, if the 
vocalism of ’ü┤’üź’ü▓’ü░’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ is old), gen. *tr’īźp-h2-o’īüs. This would yield PArm. *t

title: oak
snippet:
EMANTICSSEMANTICS The tree-name seems to have, thus, two basic meanings: 1) a kind of 
oak, the holm-oak or the evergreen oak ( Quercus Ilex ), a native of Italy and other 
Mediterranean countries; 2) cedar, pine. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM "Bar’īćgirk` hayoc`" (see above), NHB (1: 806a), and U/lmidtildeurikean (see HAB 2: 
172a) treat t`e/lmidtildeaw’åÜ as identical with or a kind of t`e/lmidtildei (note also the description of 
t`e/lmidtildeo’īä’åÜ by informants from ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde as a kind of t`e/lmidtildei), assuming, apparently, an 
etymological identity. This is accepted by J’īīahukyan (1987: 145) with some 
reservation, and by P. Friedrich (a pud Mallory/Adams 1997: 178b), where te/lmidtildeo’īä’åÜ is 
represented as meaning `wood', which is not true. AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 172a), however, 
leaves the origin of t`e/lmidtildeaw’åÜ open. Olsen (1999: 938) represents t`e/lmidtildeo’īä’åÜ as meaning 
`oak' or `pine' and as a word of unknown origin. 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 380) mentions t`e/lmidtilde-aw’åÜ as the only example of the suffix -aw’åÜ, 
and represents a separate entry for the suffix -o’åÜ found in the adjective dandal-o’åÜ vs. 
danda/lmidtilde `slow', etc. 
Perhaps ptel╔Öw- + -’åÜ-i (cf. Myc. pte-re-wa), see s.v. mori/*mo(r)-’åÜ. For this and 
for the suffix -aw’åÜ in general see 2.3.1.

*t`er- < *pter- `feather; wing'; see there for discussion and references. 
The etymological relation between t`it`er’īćn `butterfly', t`ir’īć- `to fly', and t`er(t`)

`to flutter, tremble, vibrate' (see Malxaseanc` 2, 1944: 127c, 130-132) referring to 
birds or butterflies is common in dialects and Modern Armenian. A couple of

The Tigranakert meaning, I think, allows to consider another possible cognate,

(q.v.).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Together with o/lmidtildeork (i-stem in Philo) `smooth, polished' (Bible+), derived 
from PIE *le/orgw-, cf. MIr. lerg f. `sloping expanse, hill-side, bank, plain, surface' < 
*lerga’īä, less-lergg `pasture', NIr. learg `a plain; field', MWelsh llwrw `track, trail, 
path', etc.; the initial o- in o/lmidtildeork is traced to *po- [Lide’īün 1906: 60-64; HAB 2: 277; 
3: 556; Pokorny 1959: 679; J’īīahukyan 1987: 136]. Makaev (1974: 59-60) considers 
the correspondence "more than doubtful" and proposes a derivation from *(s)legw-ro- 
< PIE *sleig- `slimy; to glide' (on which see Pokorny 1959: 663-664). 
The fact that the word occurs only in Armenian and Celtic casts doubt on the 
etymology. Admittedly, one needs a third cognate to consider the connection as 
certain. However, I see no other signific ant reasons to abandon the etymology. The 
semantic relationship `smooth, polished' : `f lat surface, plain, pastureland, field' is 
unobjectionable, cf., e.g., tap`(-) `flat, plain, smooth' : `field, plain', `pastureland' 
(cf. tuarac-a-tap`, dial. naxr-a-tap`, etc.; see s.v. place-name Tuaracatap

’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL luc `yoke' and lcem `to yoke' are dialectally ubiquitous. In ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, luc also 
refers to `the beam of a balance of which the scales are suspended' [HAB 2: 301b]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since long, linked with Skt. yuga’īü- n. `yoke, team, race, tribe' (RV+), Gr. 
’ü║’üĄ’ü¦’ü»’Ć»’ü« n. (also ’ü║’üĄ’ü¦’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ m.) `yoke of a plough of a carriage; beam of the balance; the 
constellation Libra', Lat. iugum n. `yoke (for oxen), team; pair (of horses etc.)', etc. 
(see HAB 2: 301). The initial l- has been explained by influence of *loyc- : luc-anem 
`to unbind, loosen; to dissolve, liquidate; to absolve', q.v. [Bugge 1893: 8-9; 
J’īīahukyan 1982: 40-41, 57, cf. 21339; 1987: 173]. See also s.v. leard `liver' and 2.1.7. 
Some of cognate languages have derivatives in *-lo- or *-leh2-: Skt. yugala- m., 
yugala’īä- f. `pair, couple', Lat. iugula f. `a part of the constellation Orion, Orion's belt, 
a short line of three bright stars across the middle of Orion' [Scherer 1953: 222-223],

Gu’īłrtelsterne'; see s.v. luc `yoke'.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Scheftelowitz (1904-05: 312), connected with Lat. caedo’īä `to cut; to 
hew, lop, fell; to slaughter; to murder', as well as MHG heie, hei f. `Rammblock', 
MDutch heien `schlagen, rammen', perhaps also Skt. khida’īüti `to press down' [HAB 
2: 325a; Pokorny 1959: 917; J’īīahukyan 1987: 147, 191; Clackson 1994: 224112]. The 
initial x- of the Armenian points to IE *kH- (see Kortlandt 2003: 1). The 
etymological connection, though considered "not compelling" by Olsen (1999: 211), 
seems to be acceptable (see Schrijver 1991: 266-267, who restores *kh2ei- and 
excludes Skt. (s)khida’īüti). 
There are other Armenian words which are undoubtedly related with xayt`, 
though the ablaut alternations are not quite clear (see HAB 2, s.v.v.; J’īīahukyan 1987: 
147, 191; on xit` see also Olsen 1999: 210), viz.: 
xit`, o-stem `pain, colic, twinge; rock, reef; (Paterica+) crocodile', xt`em `to bite;

title: semantics
snippet:
SSEMANTICSSEMANTICS The dialectal meanings `orphan', `young (man)', are remarkable. The 
basic semantics is `rough, stony, uncultiv ated, abandoned (place)', from which two 
meanings are developed: `step-, alien'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 2: 407b. 
J’īīahukyan (1967: 117) proposed a connection with OIc. skratti `Zauberer, Troll', 
skrydda `alte Lederjacke', Germ. *skrattaz `Schrat, Waldteufel', Lith. skriaudu’īĆs 
'beleidigend, kr’ćżnkend, klagend; rei’ć¤end, flie’ć¤end; rauh, steil', etc., from PIE 
*(s)ker- `schrumpfen, runzeln, Schorf, Krus te, vertrocknet, mager', which is 
uncertain. From the same root he (op. cit. 146-147) derives also kord `unploughed 
(land, ground)' (q.v.). In 1987: 317, J’īīahukyan rejects the comparison with Hitt. 
ßĖ½artuwa- `generation' in view of the semantic difference.

source: Martirosyan, H. (2011), Studies in Armenian Etymology
title: sxtor
snippet:
ater form, viz. sxtor, marked in NHB and HAB as `dialectal', is 
widespread in dialects, whereas the older form xstor is restricted to Aslanbek 
(extreme NW) and ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, Goris (extreme SE) [HAB 2: 428a]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 428a) connects with Gr. ’ü│’ü½’ü»’Ć»’ü▓ (’ü» )’üż’ü»’ü« n. `garlic' and Alb. 
hu’īürdhe’īł, also hu’īüdhe’īłr (Schriftsprache) f. `garlic'. As AŌĆ░ar’īćyan points out, the 
comparison with the Greek seems to be sugge sted already in NHB 1: 988c; 2: 718b. 
According to J’īīahukyan (1987: 302), we may be dealing with common (probably 
independent) borrowings. 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan's etymology has largely remained unknown to Indo-Europeanists, with a 
few exceptions (e.g. Mann 1963: 172). The Greek and Albanian are usually taken 
together, without a mention of the Armenian [Frisk 2: 738; Pokorny 1959: 941; 
Demiraj 1997: 204-205]. Similarly, Beekes (2000: 21) states that the word occurs 
only in Greek and Albanian. 
Pokorny (ibid.) derives the Greek and Albanian 
*skor-d- from PIE *(s)ker- `to 
cut', "nach den gespaltenen Wurzelknollen". The Armenian form is troublesome, 
however, and one agrees with Olsen (1999: 936) in that Arm. xstor/sxtor "cannot 
simply be derived from *ske/ord-, so we are probably faced with a cultural loan". 
This seems to be a word of Mediterranean origin.

ckson 1994: 129. The original PArm. paradigm can be reconstructed as follows: 
NSg *ce’īül-u, obl. *cal-ah-. Arm. *ca/lmidtildeu- must have generalized the vocalism of the 
oblique stem. 
For an extensive philological and ETYM ological discussion I refer to Clackson 
(1994: 126-132), who, however, suggests an old u-stem with NSg *-o’īäu(s). 
Gr. ’ü¦’üĪ’ü¼’ü©’Ć»’ü«’ü© `calm' and ’ü¦’ü¼ ’ü©’Ć»’ü«’ü© `eyeball', perhaps also ’ü¦’üź’ü¼’üĪ’Ć»’üĘ `to shine' as in Iliad 
19.362-3, may point to an original root meaning `shine'; for the semantic connection 
between `shine' and `laugh' cf. Latin verb renideo’īä `shine' : `laugh', and Engl. beam 
[Clackson 1994: 131]. Here we may be dealing with a synaesthetic transfer from the 
visual perception to the aspect of hearing or mood (cf. Arutjunjan 1983: 290; the 
appurtenance of some cognates mentioned here is uncertain). 
The root *ca/lmidtilde- is seen in ca/lmidtildeel `to deride, laugh at' (HHB), ca/lmidtilde-k-u `buffoon' (John 
Chrysostom), ca/lmidtilde-bast `laughable', if these forms are reliable, as well as in ci-ca/lmidtilde 
`laughter' (q.v.) [HAB 2: 439a]. NHB (1: 1001c, 1003c, 1015c) suggested a 
connection between not only 
ca/lmidtilder and cica/lmidtilde, but also with ca/lmidtildeik `flower' and ja/lmidtilde 
`derision, mockery' (see s.v.v.). For a possible dialectal evidence for the root *ca/l

offer no other example of IE origin in Armenian. Nevertheless, the pattern does exist

positive on the example of siser’īćn `chickpea' vs. Lat. cicer etc. Here (p. 5) he notes 
that *g^oi-g^ar-n- is possible, "though it smacks root etymology". Against the 
etymology he (ibid.) also argues that "swallows are perhaps not best known for their 
lung power". One may disagree with this.

*kakalay : `walnut; testicle' (Karin) [AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 540a]; `testicle' (Polis, K`/lmidtildei,

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (1913: 540a) compares with Georg. k’īŻak’īŻali `piece' and Kurd. /kaklŽĄ/ 
`the kernel of the walnut', not specifying the nature of the relationship. According to 
’ēüap`anc`yan (1961: 90; 1975: 369), the Armenian has been borrowed from 
Laz-Megrelian. 
Arm. *kakal and the Kartvelian forms (Georg. k’īŻak’īŻal- `walnut', Laz k’īŻak’īŻal- 
`walnut', Megr. k’īŻak’īŻal- `grain; piece', etc.) may be treated as a reduplication of *kal-; 
cf. dial.*popok`, see also s.v. kokov-. In that case, *kal- `round small object; walnut, 
etc.' may be related with the PIE word for `acorn' ( *gwlh2-(e)n-; cf. Alb. goge’īłl f. 
'acorn; small and round object', if indeed belonging to this IE werd); see s.v. ka/lmidtildein 
`acorn'. 
Since the form *kakal is found in a number of dialects mostly in the meaning 
`walnut' whereas *kakal-ay mainly refers to `testicle', one may treat the latter as a 
dual or collective in -ay. 
Perhaps unrelated with dial. (Agulis, ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, Lor’īći, ’ēüazax) *ka/lmidtildea/lmidtilde `unripe, green 
walnut to make sweets with', q.v.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymological attempt is recorded in HAB 2: 547b. 
I tentatively propose a connection with Russ. grab `hornbeam', Lith. skru’īüoblas 
`hornbeam', skir~pstas `elm', Lat. carpinus `hornbeam', etc.; perhaps also Hitt. 
GIS’īīkarpina- `a kind of tree' (see P. Friedrich 1970: 99-106; P. Friedrich apud 
Mallory/Adams 1997: 273; Schrijver 1991: 430). If Lith. skir~pstas `elm' is indeed 
related, it can help to elucidate the semantic shift seen in the Armenian, cf. Slav. 
*bersto- `elm' and Arm. bart-i `poplar/aspen' (q.v.) from PIE *bhrHg^- `birch'. 
In view of anomalous correspondences and limited spread, this tree-name may be 
of substratum origin. 
Alternatively, Arm. karb can be linked with Hitt. GIS’īīharaw- `poplar, aspen' (on 
which see Gamkrelidze/Ivanov 1984, 2: 636, with refer.). This is semantically better, 
but formally very difficult.

’ēüaraba/lmidtilde - Ar’īćtial; if the meaning `to sing' is directly comparable to the IE cognates, 
one should treat this as a semantic archaism preserved in ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde and Ar’īćtial rather 
than a shared innovation.

`id.', Lith. gru’īüodas `frozen dirt or earth', Lat. grand’Ä┐, -inis f. `hail, hail-storm', etc. 
Tervi’åÜean (see HAB) and Meillet (1898: 280) independently interpreted the 
Armenian form from reduplicated *ka-krut < *ga-gro’īädo- (cf. mamul etc., see 2.3.2), 
through regular metathesis. This is largely accepted, see HAB 2: 556a; Pokorny 
1959: 406; J’īīahukyan (1987: 126, from *g╔Ö-gro’īädo-). Hu’īłbschmann (1899: 48) is 
sceptical about *ka-krut > karkut for unspecified reasons. Rasmussen (1999: 
153-154) assumes *gr’īź-gro’īühd-i- > *kar-k(r)ut-i, through dissimilation rather than 
metathesis. 
The PIE root is reconstructed with an internal laryngeal: *groHd- or *greh3d-; the 
Latin may be derived from *grH-n-d- or *greh2-n-d-, with a nasal infix [Schrijver 
1991: 223]. Rasmussen (1999: 153) assumes *gra’īäd-n-. 
The root structure with two voiced stops is impossible in PIE. In this particular 
case this restriction is perhaps invalid since we may be dealing with an 
onomatopoeia. One can also consider the following alternativ

[HAB 2: 582b; Davt`yan 1966: 395], with a vocalic assimilation. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Pedersen (1906: 398, 400 = 1982: 176, 178) derives from the PIE word for 
`two' restoring *dwoik^o-, next to *dwouk^a’īä- > Arm. koys `side'. This is not accepted 
by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 582a), and the word is mostly viewed as of unknown origin 
[J’īīahukyan 1987: 269; 1990: 72 (sem. field Nr 13); Olsen 1999: 963]. Kortlandt 
(1989: 48, 50 = 2003: 92, 95) is more positive and takes the word as another case 
reflecting the development 
*dw- > Arm. k- (on this see 2.1.22.6). 
The semantic relationship `side, part, region' : `half' is possible, cf. Skt. a’īürdha- 
`side, part, region' : ardha’īü- `half' (RV+). However, this etymology is improbable in 
view of the absence of cognate forms which would confirm the reconstructton. 
Furthermore, koys `side' (q.v.) is an Iranian loan and has nothing to do with the word 
for `two'. The same perhaps holds for ke’īäs, though no Iranian correspondent is 
indicated [Viredaz 2003: 64 22]. Earlier, J’īīahukyan (1967: 143) suggested a derivation 
from PIE *ken- `to rub, scrape off', which is impossible.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Usually (Meillet, Pedersen, Kortlandt, etc.) derived from *dui-sk- (cf. OHG 
zwisk `double'); for the discussion see Kort landt 2003: 91-95; Olsen 1999: 269-271. 
For objections on the semantics see Viredaz 2003: 64 22. Discussing the 
counter-evidence for the development *dw- > Arm. -rk-, Beekes (2003: 200) 
considers kic` < *dui-sk- "most convincing" and takes erkic`-s `twice, again' (see 
s.v. erku `two') as `modernized' after the new form of the word for `two' (i.e. erku) 
and points out that kic` "therefore developed a more remote meaning (from `*two 
together')". 
The derivation from *gwi-sk^- [J’īīahukyan 1987: 249] < PIE *gwei- 
`zusammendr’ćżngen, einschlie’ć¤en , einpferchen' (cf. OIc. kv’ē¦a `einpferchen' etc.) is 
improbable since it is semantically remote, and the status of the PIE word is 
uncertain. Elsewhere (op. cit. 609-610) J’īīahukyan treats kic` as an ECauc borrowing, 
cf. Tindi ą║ąĖčåIą▓ `knot' etc.

s.v. *keŌĆ░`-i `birch'. 
J’īīahukyan (1975: 37) mentions kiw among cases displaying absence of 
palatalization of velars. If this word is indeed related with the PIE verb for `live', the 
absence of palatalization might be explaine d by the influence of the etymologically

Ali’åÜan (1895: 3201; see also J’īīahukyan 1967: 270157) noted the resemblance with Irish 
oinsean, uinsean. [What is the origin of the Irish word? From the same *u’ī»ing^- ?]. 
On the semantics of the Ossetic form see s.v. t`/lmidtildek`i `maple' (from `elm'?). 
A PIE *u’ī»ing^- would yield Arm. *ginc/j. One would expect, thus, *g(i)ncni or 
*g(i)njni. Sasun knjni (see above), possibly from an older *gnj/cni, is remarkable in

The meaning `walnut' is also found in: S’īīatax k/cturnk`y/cturnv [M. Muradyan 1962: 213a], 
Moks k/cturnk`y/cturnv [Orbeli 2002: 273]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 618b) considers the resemblance with Ital. coglioni 
`testicles' (NHB) as accidental and leaves the origin of kokov open. 
One may compare with Arm. ╔Ön-koy-z and Pers. go’īäz `walnut', interpreting them 
as *gou-z = *gou- + -z "plant-suffix" (on the latter see 2.3.1). [See also *koŌĆ░-]. With 
reduplication: *go-gou- > kokov. For the semantic field (cf. also Monchi-Zadeh 
1990: 11-12 Nr2) and reduplication see s.v. *kakal(ay) (dial.) `walnut; testicle' and 
below; 
If the absence of the final -v in dial. g/cturng/cturn is not due to loss, one may treat koko-v 
`testicles' as from *koko `round; walnot; etc.' with the dual suffix *-v(i), on which 
see the following. 
The form ko’īäklvin `testicles' (attested in Physiologus) may have resulted from 
contamination with kakal `walnut; testicles' (q.v.). Alternatively: *kokol- (cf. kakal) 
+ dual *-vi- > *koko(l)vi-. Note also kl-or `round'. For the semantics cf. Pahl., 
NPers. gund `testicle', Xura’īäsa’īän’ē¦ Pers. gond `testicle' vs. *gund- `round' (see 
MacKenzie 1971: 38; Monchi-Zadeh 1990: 63), cf. Arm. Gund. 
Further, note Alb.

If the connection is accepted, it cannot explain the whole semantic field. One 
needs to establish the internal etymology firs t. The basic meaning is `to beat, break'. 
One may therefore derive *koŌĆ░- from koc- `to beat', `to lament by beating one's 
breast' (both Bible+) assuming a reduplicated present in o-grade with the present 
suffix *-i’ī»e- (see 2.1.22.1 and 2.2.6.1).

`to say, speak, call, name': Goth. qi’ćŠan, OIc. kve’ć░a, OEngl. cwe’ćŠan, etc. He (op. cit. 
69) is sceptical about the appurtenance of Skt. ga’īüdati `to speak articulately, say, 
relate, tell' < *gad-. Meillet (1936: 108; 1950: 110) accepts the connection and posits 
a *i’ī»e-present: *gwot-i’ī»e- > koŌĆ░`em (see 2.1.22.1 and 2.2.6.1). 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 635) rejects the etymology and treats Arm. koŌĆ░`em as an 
onomatopoeic word comprising the elements k- and -ŌĆ░`-, cf. kanŌĆ░`-, ka(r)kaŌĆ░`- (q.v.), 
etc. However. the onomatopoeic character of a word should not automatically 
exclude the possibility of internal comparison. 
The etymology is overall accepted [Pokorny 1959: 480-481; J’īīahukyan 1975: 38; 
1982: 62, 171; Greppin 1993: 16, 19; Kortlandt 1994: 27 = 2003: 104]. The 
appurtenance of the Sanskrit, though accepted by Pokorny and J’īīahukyan, is

see 2.1.33.1. 
A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 105-106) derives ker’īć, kor and dial. kor’īć from QIE *ger-s- (cf. OHG 
kresan `to creep, crawl' etc.; for the root see s.v. kart` `fish-hook'). J’īīahukyan (1987: 
125) accepts this etymology of ker’īć and *kor’īć, but separates kur from these (see 
above). However, the Germanic cognates are remote both formally and semantically. 
Uncertain. See also s.v.v. kart` `fish-hook', kr’īćt`unk` `back', etc. 
See also s.v.v. kor(ŌĆ░) `scorpion' and korŌĆ░ `vulture'.

(ground); hard' [HAB 2: 646b]; also in Xotorj’īīur [Yu’åÜamXotorj’īī 1964: 472]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 646) rejects all the etymological attempts including the 
comparison with Germ. hart etc. and the place-name Korduk` (Tervi’åÜyan). J’īīahukyan 
(1985a: 367; 1987: 432, 598; 1990: 68.), though with hesitation, treats Arm. kord 
and its Kartvelian correspondents as borrowed from Urart. quldi/e(ni) `id.'. Olsen 
(1999: 953) mentions kord in her list of words of unknown origin. 
Bearing in mind the alternation k : x , one may try a connection with xort` 
`stepson; `hard, rough, stony' (q.v.)13. 
 
13 Alternatively, one may derive kord from *ghordh-: cf. Lith. gar~das m. `fence, enclosure, 
(sheep's) pen', OCS gradčŖ m. `stronghold, town, garden', etc.); cf. also *ghor-t-: Gr. ’üŻ’ü»’Ć»’ü▓’ü┤’ü»’ü¬ 
m. `enclosed place; farmyard, in which the cattle were kept; pasturage; fodder', Lat. hortus m. 
`garden; (pl.) pleasure-grounds', Osc. hu’īürz, acc. hu’īürtu’īüm `lucus, Hain' (< `Einz’ćżunung'), etc. 
The basic meaning of the Armenian would be, then, `(enclosed) pasture-land, pen, etc.'. The 
form *ghordh- might give *kord- through Grassmann's Law (see 2.1.24.1).

which is not convincing. 
The derivation from kor `curved' is worth of consideration. Compare also korŌĆ░ 
`curved, crooked, rough' (Grammarians), and *kor(ŌĆ░) `scorpion', q.v. For the 
semantic shift `curved, bent' > `vulture' (i.e. `having a curved beak, hook-beaked') 
cf. Gr.

attested: GDSg krak-i (E/lmidtildei’åÜe’īä, ’ēüazar P`arpec`i, etc.), AblSg i -krak-e’īä (Eusebius of 
Caesarea), LocSg i krak-i (Bible, see Astuacaturean 1985: 814a) `fire'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Ubiquitous in dialects. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Lide’īün (1906: 123-124) proposed a connection with Germ. Kohle `coal' and 
Ir. gu’īüal `coal' (< *ge/ou-lo-), assuming an interchange of the suffix *-lo- : *-ro-, or a 
reshaping of Arm. *kul- to *kur- due to influence of hur `fire'; see also Pokorny 
1959: 399; J’īīahukyan 1987: 126, 169 (with reservation); Klug e/Seebold 1989: 388. 
This etymology is improbable since the explanation of -r- is not convincing, and the 
ending -ak points rather to Iranian origin. Besides, the Germanic etc. are probably 
related with Skt. jvar/l- `to burn, glow': jva’īülana- m. `fire', j’ģ½rn’īŻi’īü- f. `glow, glowing 
fire', jva’īäla’īü `coal' [Lubotsky 1988: 38; 1992: 262-263], Pers. zuva’īäl `a live coal, 
firebrand' and Oss. ’ć”vzaly/u `coal' from Iran. *zua’īär [Cheung 2002: 167] and, 
therefore, presuppose an initial *g^-, which would yield Arm. c- (see also s.v. acu/lmidtilde 
`coal'). 
More probably, krak is an Iranian loan, cf. Pers. ku’īära `furnace, fire-place', etc. 
[Eilers 1974: 317-318, cf. 321; Ivanov 1976: 81 52]; on Sem. and other forms see 
Cabolov 1, 2001: 572, and especially HAB 4: 595, s.v. Arm. k`(u)ray

possible than *ki

curved', ’ü¦’üĄ ~’ü▓’ü»’ü¬ m. `rounding, circle'; Arm. kur’īćn `back' (q.v.); etc., though the

According to AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 684b), Laz k’īŻur `heel', borrowed from Armenian, 
shows that the root of krukn is *kur. In view of Urart. qur╔Ö and Hurrian ukr╔Ö `foot', 
which, according to Diakonoff/Starostin ( 1986: 57), are connected with Proto-East-

hoof', cf. also ArŌĆ░i k’īŻwiri `animal's foot', etc.), the relationship between the 
Armenian and Laz words seems to be deeper, however. 
See also s.v. armukn `elbow'.

V. Ar’īćak`elyan (1979: 43-44) argues that both in literature and dialects ku/lmidtilde 
basically refers to `rope, cord' rather than `fold, twisting, plait'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Bugge (1889: 42; 1892: 457) derives ku/lmidtilde `Doppelung, das Doppelte' from 
*duoplo- (cf. Lat. duplus etc.). AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 2: 657a) does not accept the 
etymology leaving the origin of the wo rd open. The connection is adopted by 
Pedersen (1906: 398 = 1982: 176), Kortlandt (1989: 48, 50 = 2003: 92, 95), Beekes 
(2003: 200). 
Since the basic meaning of ku/lmidtilde seems to be `rope, cord, string, etc.' (see above, 
also V. Ar’īćak`elyan 1979: 43-44), and in view of the resemblance with k`u/lmidtilde `(plaited) 
thread' (Bible+); dial. also `cord; lace', I consider the derivation of ku/lmidtilde from 
*duoplo- as improbable. The connection between ku/lmidtilde and k`u/lmidtilde has been suggested by 
Dervischjan (1877: 37-38). The alternation k : k` favours a loan origin. 
See also s.v.v. erku `two', erkiw/lmidtilde `fear', and 2.1.22.6.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM A connection with Lat. quie’īäsco’īä `to rest' was suggested by Pedersen (1905: 
219 = 1982: 81). AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 35b) mentions Pedersen's comparison but leaves 
the origin of the word open. Meillet (1936a) independently suggests the same 
comparison convincingly deriving PArm. *hangi- from *sm’īź-kwiH-, cf. Lat. quie’īäs, 
-e’īätis f. `rest, quiet, peace; sleep; death', quie’īäsco’īä `to rest'. For the absence of 
palatalization of the labiovelar after nasal he compares Arm. hing `five' from 
*penkwe. For references and discussion on hangi-st, -ean see Olsen 1999: 480-482. 
Lat. quie’īäs, -e’īätis derives from *kwieh1-ti-, cf. Av. ’åÜa’īäiti- f. `happiness', OPers. 
’åÜiya’īäti- f. `Glu’īłck, Glu’īłckseligkeit, Wohlfahrt', Av. ’åÜya’īäto’īä `happy' < *-to-, etc. 
[Brandenstein/Mayrhofer 1964: 143; Schrijver 1991: 140]; cf. Arm. ’åÜat, an Iranian 
loan [HAB 3: 498-499]. 
Remarkably, *sm’īź-kwiH- is found also in Iranian languages: Oss. ’ć”ncad `quiet, 
tranquil, quietly' from *ham-ŌĆ░ya’īäta, Sogd. 'nc'y `to stay, remain', and Khwarezm.
