`without, far from' < * sn’īź-te’īür) and *tane- < *da’īä-ne/o- `teilen', which is unacceptable. 
A/lmidtildeayan (1974: 95-98) links the words with yatak `bottom', (h)und `seed' and hunj 
`mowing, harvest(-time)' and traces *hawt- to PIE *peu- `to hit', pres. *p╔Öu-d-, cf. 
Lith. pja’īüuju, pja’īüuti (*p’ÉĄu’ī»i’ī»’Ä┐) `to cut, mow', Lat. pavi’Ä┐, -’ē¦re `to hit', pav’ē¦mentum n. 
`paved surface or floor', from d-pres., probably: pudeo’īä `to be ashamed', etc. The 
form *hawt is taken, thus, as original, and the loss of the -w- in hat is not explained. 
Olsen (1999: 90) mentions hatanem as "etymologically unclear". She (op. cit. 17), 
like practically everyone, accepts the internal connecton between hat `grain, seed; 
piece' and hatanem `to cut'. 
The best etymology seems to be the one proposed by *Poetto (1976; see also

Morani (1991: 176-178). According to it, hat, o-stem `gr

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology in HAB 3: 76a. 
J’īīahukyan (1979: 27-28) derives from hecan... (from PIE *sed- `to sit'). He treats 
hecan-oc` `a kind of winnowing-fan' (Bible+) as a derivative of hecan `beam, log'. 
In my view, this is parallel to the derivation of gerandi `scythe; sickle' from geran 
`beam, log` (see s.v.v.). Note that both geran (a-stem) and hecan display the same 
suffix -an, and the same semantic development (`beam, log' > `a kind of 
meteorological phenomenon'). 
Olsen (1999: 299, 951) represents hecan as an etymologically obscure word.

The genitive hec`-i implies that the word had either i- or a-stem. If i-stem (as 
stated in NHB), one may link hec` with other formations with the suffixal -c` (< PIE 
*-sk-) like harc`, i-stem `question, inquiry' (Agat`ange/lmidtildeos+) and c`oyc` (i-stem)

assumed that *pel-k^- is a form of *plek^- `to plait': Gr. ’ü░’ü¼’üź’Ć»’ü½’üĘ , OHG flehtan, `to 
plait'; Russ. plesti, etc. [Gamkrelidze/Ivanov 1984, 2: 706 1]. For the semantic shift 
`to wind, plait' > `felloe' see 3.9.4. 
Arm. hec` can be derived from *pelk^-sk- (for *-sk- see above) or a PArm. 
secondary nominative *pelk^-s (cf. 2.2.1.2). Both would result in *he/lmidtildec`. For the loss 
of the lateral followed by an affricate -c` see 2.1.22.9.

`avalanche' under the word hiws, i-stem `plait' (q.v.) and interprets it as follows:

into account the forms with the u-vocalism in Xotorj’īīur etc., as well as the meaning 
`snow-storm' of Ararat f/cturnsan (see HAB 3: 315a). However, the former alternative 
seems better both formally and semantically. 
The idea that the abundance of snow is expressed through `weaving, plaiting' is 
corroborated by the following spectacular passage from P`awstos Buzand 3.14 
(1883=1984: 32 L-4f; transl. Garso/FL872an 1989: 87): yor’å×am kuteal dizeal zmecut`iwn

title: hiwsis

(wo-stem) `north; northern wind'. 
Bible+. Spelled also as hiwsiws(i), hisis(i), etc. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Preserved in Mu’åÜ, Sebastia, Xarberd ( hisis), Karin, Axalc`xa, T`iflis, Ararat, 
Salmast [HAB 3: 102a]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 3: 102a. 
S. Petrosyan (1977: 215) derives from PIE *seu- `left', also mentioning Russ. 
se’īüver `North' and Lith. ’åÜia’īüure’īć `North'. This etymology is uncertain, however, since 
no details are discussed. The Balto-Slavic forms cannot be related since Lith. ’åÜ- 
presupposes PIE *k^-; they belong to a different PIE word (see s.v. c`urt `cold'). 
J’īīahukyan (1986-1987) derives hiwsis `north' from *seukoi-k^i(y)o-, a compound 
of PIE *seuk-e/oi- (the locative form of *seuk-o-, cf. Lith. su’īĆkti 'drehen, wenden, 
kehren, betr’ć╝gen, bet’ćČren', Slav. sukati `to turn'; see s.v. hiwsem `to weave, plait') 
and PIE *k^ei- `se trouver' (cf. Gr. ’ü½’üź’ü® ~’üŁ’üĪ’ü® ; see s.v. se’īär `love'), the basic meaning 
being "qui se trouve a’īü l'oppose’īü". He treats it as "co~te’īü inverse", in opposition with 
haraw `south', etymologically "co~te’īü du devant" (q.v.). Olsen (1999: 960) lists hiwsis 
among the words of unknown origin and does not mention Petrosyan's and 
J’īīahukyan's etymologies.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 271a). He points

accidental. 
J’īīahukyan (1972: 282) compares with Avest. mati- `Vorsprung des Gebirges', 
which derives from PIE *mn-t-, cf. Lat. mons, GSg montis `mountain', Alb. mat m. 
`Ufer; Sandstrand' (see Demiraj 1997: 50, 256). 
I wonder whether it is not identic with matn1 `finger' (q.v.). The semantic transfer 
from body-part terms into topographical ones is trivial. Note that in one of the 
passages from Geoponica matn-er occurs with ko/lmidtilde-er, which actually is identic with 
ko/lmidtilde `rib', and tap`er. A comparable semantic relationship may be seen in PIE 
*pr-sth2- `standing before': Lith. pir~’åÜtas `finger', OCS prčīstčŖ `finger' : Skt. pr’īźs’īŻß╣Łha’īü-

title: mglamandi
snippet:
DIALDIALDIALDIAL I find the word only in Goris m╔Ökl╔Ömandi < *mglamandi `spider-web' 
[Margaryan 1975: 440a]. There are also forms with a final -l, see 
Martirosyan/’ēüaragyozyan FW 2003, Goris and ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymological attempt is known to me. 
I propose to treat the word as follows: *mgl- `mould/Schimmel' (see s.v. 
*mglim1) + -a- + *mandi `yarn or web', probably a -di- < *-tii’ī»V- formation based on 
manem `to spin' (class., widespread in dialects, among them also in Goris). The 
voicing *t > d after -n- and -r- is regular; see s.v.v. anj’īīrdi, ordi, spand (etc.), all being 
composed of the same suffix. Compare also sard, i- stem `spider' (q.v.). The 
spider-web is taken to be, then, a mould-like yarn/web, which is quite conceivable. 
If this etymology is accepted, one should consider *mglamandi as archaic, since 
the formation is old, and Goris only has *mglim2 `to scorch, singe' (in the compound 
*mglahot), which can eventually be connected to*mglim1. 
Alternatively, one might think that the first component of *mglamandi 
`spider-web' is *mglim2 `to scorch, singe', having developed into `(sooty) 
spider-web'; cf. unj’īī3 `soot' (q.v.), which refers to to the (sooty) spider-web in 
’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, Hin J’īīu/lmidtildea, probably also Goris and S’īīamaxi. The semantic relationship `soot'

title: merjim

merj merj merj merj `near', merjimmerjimmerjimmerjim, merjenam merjenam merjenam merjenam `to approach, touch' (Bible+). 
For biblical attestations and philol ogical discussion see Clackson 1994: 150, 
230207. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Meillet and others, connected with Gr. ’üŁ’üź’Ć»’üŻ’ü▓’ü® `as far as; up to, about, 
nearly; until; as long as, wilst' (see HAB 3: 308-309). PArm. *merji is seen in 
merjenam < *merji-anam (see HAB ibid., and especially Clackson 1994: 230 207). 
Adontz (1937: 10-11) assumes *me-g^hr-i, a compound of *me- `in' and the locative 
of the word for `hand', thus `at hand'. In view of Hitt. ke’åÜ’åÜar `hand' (cf. loc. 
ki’åÜ(’åÜe)ri), one has to start with *me-g^hsr-i (Frisk 2: 222; sceptical: Hamp 1983: 7). 
For a thorough discussion see Clackson 1994: 150-152. 
The proto-form *me-g^hsr-i helps to explain the absence of depalatalization of 
*-g^h- before *-r- in Armenian [Kortlandt 1985b: 10; 1986: 42 = 2003: 58, 71; Beekes

ModArm. and dialects, seems to continue PIE formula *mens- dheh1- `to put in the 
mind', replacing the first member by mit < *me’īäd-.

snippet:
r-i > Svedia mura as a derivation of our MArm. mor `tarantula'. For this 
i-form cf. perhaps Georg. morieli `scorpion' which, according to G. Asatur (p.c. 
apud HAB 3: 347b), is borrowed from Arm. mor `tarantula'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM ’ēüap`anc`yan (1927: 108; 1961: 359-360) derives from IE *mer- `to die' 
linking with Pers. ma’īär `snake'. AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 347b) does not accept this 
etymology and leaves the origin of the Armenian word open. 
As we have seen, MArm. mor/morm `tarantula' is dialectally represented in 
extreme peripheries: SW (Svedia/Syria) mor vs. E (Ararat, J’īīu/lmidtildea) morm. The word 
may thus be old. 
M. A/lmidtildeabekyan (1980: 162-167) proposed a connection with mrj’īīiwn `ant' (q.v.), 
cf. especially dialectal forms such as Lor’īći m/cturnrm/cturnnj’īī etc. I accept this connection in 
terms which will be discussed furthe r. More closely, I think, Arm. morm `tarantula' 
may be linked with Gr. ’üŹ’ü»’ü▓’üŁ’üĘ’Ć» , -’ü»’Ć»’ü»’ü¬ - ’ü»’üĄ ~’ü¬ , ’üŹ’ü»’ü▓’üŁ’üĘ’Ć» v, -’ü»’Ć»’ü«’ü»’ü¬ f. `she-monster, bogy' 
(also used by nurses to frighten children), generally `bugbear', and Lat. form’ē¦do’īä, inis 
f. `fear, terror; a thing which frightens, bogy'. For the semantic relation `spider, 
insect' : `bogy, ghost' see s.v. *bo-/bu-, and 3.5.2.1. 
The Greek and Latin words are related, e ither etymologically or secondarily, with 
the word for `ant', cf. Lat.

’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Widespread in dialects, mostly as *mr’īćmr’īćal, as well as *mr’īć(r’īć)al [HAB 3: 
366a]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Identic with Lat. murmuro’īä `to hum, murmur, mutter; to roar', etc. The direct 
connection is usually rejected in view of the onomatopoeic nature of the word 
[Hu’īłbschmann 1897: 476; HAB 3: 366a; Greppin 1981b: 6]. However, this view 
cannot be maintained since the onomatopoeic nature does not automatically preclude 
the etymological connection. See also J’īīahukyan 1987: 139, 448.

Arm. yawray is treated as a native term (see Clackson 1994: 146) that has later

Two things are puzzling: the inital y- and the ending -ay. The derivation of

’äć’äć’äć’äć DIALDIALDIALDIAL Widespread in the dialects with an aspirated -p`; in Mu’åÜ and Ala’åÜkert one 
finds GSg ’åÜŽĄrb`i next to NSg ’åÜŽĄrep`; see HAB 3: 511a. Ba/lmidtildedasaryan-T`ap`alc`yan 
1958 vacat. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 511a). J’īīahukyan 
(1967: 261) connects to Russ. ŌĆ░erep `scull', ŌĆ░erpa’īüt` `to scoop, draw, ladle (out), 
ŌĆ░erpak `scoop, ladle', etc. from PIE *(s)ker-p- `to chop, cut' (see s.v. k`er-, k`er-b-, 
k`er-p`- `to scratch, chop, carve'). The comparison is interesting, but the 
phonological details are unclear. Later he (J’īīahukyan 1990: 71, sem. field 5) 
considered the word to be of unknown origin. 
The initial ’åÜ- instead of c`- or k`-, as well as the final -ep` might argue in favour of 
substratum origin: *skhereph-; see also s.v.v. ’åÜert, ’åÜe/e’īär. However, the derivation from 
PIE *(s)ker-p- seems plausible if one assumes initial metathesis *sk- > *ks- and 
ruki-rule (see 2.1.12). Thus: *kser-eph- > ’åÜerep`. In either case, the -ep` can be 
compared with another tool-name, viz. ’åÜa/lmidtildeap` `borer, gimlet'. Note the dependence of 
the vowel rbefore *ph upon the root vowel: ’åÜer-ep` vs. ’åÜa/lmidtilde-ap` (cf. 2.1.23). 
The root may be identic with see ’åÜe/e’īär ; thus: ladle made of storax-wood.

to be mistakenn, confused; to see badly', cf. ’åÜe/lmidtilde `slanting, crooked, oblique', ’åÜe/lmidtildeem

`to twist, turn'. This view is hard to accept since the assumed development *-l’īźh1C- >

1972: 388; N. MkrtŌĆ░`yan 1983: 31-32; J’īīahukyan 1987: 459, 470; Greppin 1989a: 
79].

an old culture word of Mediterranean and Near-Eastern areas. Note also another 
synonym of Mediterranean origin, viz. siser’īćn `pea' (see s.v.).

Olsen (1999: 125-126) points out that the semantic divergence between `spine' 
(something twisting or turning) and `elbow ' (something bending in an angle) is 
considerable, which seems to me exaggerated. The spine and neck can not only twist 
and turn, but also bend in an angle. Besides, the shoulder, also a bending body part, 
is semantically often related with the back (see par. XX). Note also that, in the

For the semantic relationship between `mouse and the like' and `mole' cf. 
also ambewt, wich in Xotorj’īīur means both `mole' and `field-mouse' (see s.v.). 
I conclude, that *ŌĆ░`asum probably means `blind mole-rat'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Stating that this animal is in fact unknown and mysterious to many people, 
Andreasyan (1967: 161-162) suggests a connection to Arm. j’īīasm, a hapax used in 
Anania Narekac`i (10th cent.), itself of uncertain meaning (probably `a mythic being, 
ghost') and of unknown origin (see HAB 4: 123b). Furthermore, it is semantically 
remote and phonologically incompatible. 
The animal under discussion is obviously distinct from the weasel. For the 
description of the latter I refer to Ananyan, HayKendA’åÜx 1, 1961: 163-171. In some 
respects, however, such as the size (both are smaller than the rat; pertaining to the 
weasel see Ananyan, op. cit. 164), there is a certain resemblance. If *ŌĆ░`asum refers 
indeed to the `blind mole-rat', one might add more resembling characteristics such as 
being fierce and having a (more or less) valuable fur. For the semantic relationship

One might conclude from the passage that pa/lmidtildeat2 refers to `(a kind of) 
ceremonial/solemn recitation' or `religious service performed by recitation'. 
The word is mentioned neither in NHB nor in HAB. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Probably to be connected with pa/lmidtildeat1 `entreaty, supplication; prayer' (q.v.), 
which in Joel 1.14 and 2.15 seems to refe r to `solemn assembly, religious service or 
ceremony'. 
The semantic shift `prayer' > `religious service performed by recitation' is 
typologically comparabe to that of tawn `feast' (q.v.) . The original meaning of the 
latter must have been `sacrificial meal' (cf. OIc. tafn `sacrificial animal', etc.). In the 
above-mentioned passage from "Patmut`iwn srboc` Hr’īćip`simeanc`", tawn, directly 
equated to pa/lmidtildeat2, is used with the verb kardam `to recite' and, therefore, refers to the 
religious service performed by recitation. 
Note the mountain-name Pa/lmidtildeat of the same passage. Russell (1987: 179 31) follows 
Ali’åÜan in treating Pa’åÜat as the correct reading and interprets it as *pa’åÜt-’åÜat 
`abounding in worship'. Note that the Armenian characters ’åÜ : /lmidtilde are similar. 
Eremyan (1963: 36a, 77a), too, accepts the reading Pa’åÜat identifying the mountain 
with Assyrian Pas’īŻatu and modern Ba’åÜet`-da/lmidtilde.

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM J’īīahukyan (1972: 282) compares with Skt. j’É░’īüm’É░tar- `son-in-law, husband of 
the daughter' (RV+) from PIE *g^emH-. For the phonetic side he (op. cit. 282 6) 
compares with the case of j’īīambem, implicitly and hesitantly suggesting, thus, an 
Indo-Aryan borrowing. This is uncertain, however. The loss of intervocalic -t- is an 
old feature, occurring in words of PIE origin (hayr `father' etc.), whereas the initial j’īī- 
(without consonant shift) points to a relatively young period. 
Perhaps borrowed from Persian j’īīawa’īän-mard `a young man; a generous youth; 
brave, generous, manly', j’īīu’īä-mard(um) `a liberal or generous man' (see Steingass 
376b, 379a); cf. also Arm. dialect of Ararat j’īīomard `generous' (see Nawasardeanc` 
1903: 102a). For loss of the final -d cf. argand `womb' > S’īīam’åÜadin a’īłrk`a’īłn and 
Ala’åÜkert argan (see s.v.).

One may assume that we are dealing with a noun *sal-ar- `flat stone (for paving)'

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since Bugge (1893: 24; see also Meillet 1936: 43), connected with Skt. s’īüil’É░’īü- 
`stone, rock, crag' (AV+), perhaps also with OIc. hella `flat stone' < Germ. *hallj’Ä┐n, 
hallr `stone' < *halluz, Goth. hallus `reef'; see HAB 4: 155b; Pokorny 1959: 542; 
J’īīahukyan 1987: 131 (the Germenic cognate - with a question-mark); Olsen 1999: 
100-101. For the semantic shift `stone' > `anvil' cf. Skt. a’īüs’īüman- m. `stone', Av. 
asman- `stone, heaven', Lith. akmuo~, - en~s `stone', etc. vs. Gr. ’üĪ’éö’ü½’üŁ’üĘ’ü« `anvil; 
meteoric stone; pestle'. 
The Armenian word has been borrowed into Georgian sali `rock' and sala `a flat 
roundish stone to play with' [HAB 4: 155-156]. The -a of the latter seems to point to 
PArm. *sal-a-, which matches the Sanskrit form perfectly: *k^Hl-eh2- (see J’īīahukyan 
1987: 590). In ’ēüazar P`arpec`i, however, sal has i-stem, which points to another 
feminine form: *k^Hl-ih2-. If these data prove reliable, we may be dealing with an 
interchange between *-eh2- and *-ih2- feminines. 
The Germanic form, if related, may derive from *k^Hl-n-. One wonders whether 
the Armenian district-name S

Since Anania S’īīirakac`i must have spoken a kind of proto-dialect of Karin or the 
like, it is interesting to note that Baberd is included in the Karin-speaking area. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 4: 414b. Gabikean (1952: 532) 
derives from t/lmidtildeay `child', which is highly improb able. J’īīahukyan (1967: 135; 1985: 
155-156; 1990: 70) links t/lmidtildeuk with Arm. dial. tlk`el, t/lmidtildekel `in Wasser weich werden' 
etc. comparing them with Russ. čéą╗čÅ (tlja) `Motte', from an IE root with initial *t-. 
This is not convincing, especially in view of the Armenian t- instead of the expected 
aspirated *t`-. 
I wonder if t/lmidtildeuk is somehow related with the synonymic tzruk `leech' (q.v.). I 
propose a scenario, which, however, must be verified within the chronoligacal 
framework. All the dialectal forms representing the consonant shift t > d, viz. 
Xarberd, Sebastia, and Dersim, have undergone a metathesis: d╔Örzug, d╔Örjug. I 
assume that the metathesis was a shared innovation in these closely related dialects 
rather than a recent sound change having taken place in each of these dialects 
independently. For a certain stage prior to the consonant shift, therefore, one may 
reconstruct a theoretical form like*trzuk. If the Iranian

cannot be specified. H. Muradyan (1960: 30) explains the KarŌĆ░ewan form (h)╔Ö/lmidtilde/cturn’īü/lmidtilde 
from u/lmidtildee/lmidtilde by regressive assimilation ( u/lmidtildee/lmidtilde > *u/lmidtildeu/lmidtilde) and change of the pretonic u to ╔Ö. 
One may also think of vocalic metathesis (s ee 2.1.26.4). In either case, however, one

In view of the cognate forms with a *-k-, viz. Lith. u’īüolektis, Latv. uo^lekts `ell'; 
Lith. alk’ģ½’īüne’īć `elbow', Latv. e’ī©’īĆlkuonis `elbow, bend', etc. (see s.v. olok`), one wonders 
if a PArm. *u/lmidtilde-k`- underlies u/lmidtildeuk. The unaspirated -k could be due to contamination 
with -k-ean (cf. vayr-(i)k-ean `moment'). If this is true, the word-final -k` in ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde 
h╔Ö/lmidtilde/cturn’īük` may become significant, and the internal -u- in u/lmidtildeuk should be treated as 
secondary, unless u/lmidtildeuk is from *HoHl-o’īäk. Note also the identity of the root vocalism

the district of Ayrarat". Malxasyanc` (1987: 313) renders ╔Önj’īī-in by ModArm. kalvac 
`estate'. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 605a) identifies with Georg. unj’īīi `treasure', of which 
unj’īī-eba (verb) is derived, corresponding to Arm. ganj-em in the Bible. Then he 
(ibid.) notes that he does not know whether there is a connection with unj’īī1 `depth, 
bottom' (q.v.). I think the connection is very plausible. The semantic development 
would have been `*bottom, depth, the underground' > `buried/underground treasure 
or granary'. Note that unj’īī is attested in P`awstos Buzand in various senses: `bottom, 
below', `depth', `root' (see s.v. unj’īī1), and `treasury, granary, barn' (see the passage 
above). For semantic (cf. ganj) and etymological discussion see 1.12.6. See also s.v. 
unj’īī3. 
In the passage from P`awstos, thus, Ardeans is said to be a village of the royal 
treasury or, perhaps better, of the royal granary/barn. This may be corroborated by 
the etymology of the place-name (q.v.).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymological attempt is known to me. 
The word may be related with unj’īī1 `bottom, depth' (> unj’īī2 `treasure'). Its basic 
meaning would then be `sediment/Bodensa tz' (< `settling, sinking down'); cf. mur 
`soot' vs. mrur `sediment'. Remarkably, Kr’īćzen unj’īī refers to not only `rust' ( ’å×ang), 
but also `sediment' ( mrur). The semantics is corroborated by ma/lmidtilde-unŌĆ░` `sediment of 
grain left on the bottom of a sieve' (with the first component ma/lmidtilde `sieve'), attested in 
"Oskip`orik" (probably by Grigor Tat`ewac`i, 14-15th cent.). According to HAB (3: 
604b), this compound contains unj’īī1 `bottom'. This can serve as a semantic 
intermediary between unj’īī1 `bottom' and unj’īī3 `soot (< sediment)'. Also the following 
seems relevant for the connection: unj’īī3 `soot (< sediment)' has been preserved only 
SE dialects (Goris, ’ēüaraba/lmidtilde, etc.), and unj’īī1 `bottom' is absent in dialects. However, 
the latter is found in a number of place-names located in Goris and adjacent areas 
(see s.v. K`ar(ah)unj’īī), and Grigor T`at`ewac`i (see above on ma/lmidtilde-unŌĆ░`) has lived in 
Ta

th a willow-like tree, see the material s.v.v. aygi 
`vineyard' and gi `juniper'. Compare also Gr. ’ü│’üŁ’ü® ~’ü¼’üĪ’üĖ `yew, or bindweed, or 
holm-oak' rendered by Arm. ge/lmidtildej `bindweed, convolvulus; yew-tree' (q.v.). 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymology in HAB 3: 606b. 
J’īīahukyan (1967: 255; in 1987: 141, with a question-mark) connects with Lith. 
u’īüosis `ash-tree' etc., and Arm. hac`i `ash-tree', positing *o’īäskhii’ī»a’īä-. The *-skh- (next to 
*-sk- in hac`i `ash-tree') is not clear, however. One may assume that the Armenian 
form reflects a metathesized form found also in Gr. ’ü»’Ć¦’üĖ’üĄ’Ć»’üĪ , - ’ü© `beech; spear-shaft 
made from its wood, spear'. For QIE *ks- > Arm. -’åÜ- (ruki-rule in internal position) 
see 2.1.12. Next to Cheremis o’åÜko `ash', note Erzamordvin uks(o) `elm, ash' (see 
Normier 1981: 23-24). Thus: QIE *h3ek-s-ieh2- > PArm. *ho’åÜi’īüya- > *ho’åÜi. On the 
other hand, the by-form u’åÜi, if old, points to QIE *Ho’īäks- from *HoHs- (cf. Lith. 
u’īüosis `ash-tree' etc.); see s.v.v. hac`i and hoyn. 
For the semantic shift `ash' > `storax-tree' and `(holm-)oak' the following is 
relevant. Both the ash and the storax 1) have valuable wood of which spears or other 
implements are made; 2) produce manna or gum-resin. Note that in Sasun the manna 
is found on leaves of oak-trees, and this tree is here called 
h/cturn’åÜi (which is our word)

ag on/around the shoulders' [HZ’īīHek` 5, 1966: 398 L2]; xurj’īīin╔Ö <...> /cturnsu’īłk`╔Ö k`c`- 
(ibid. 109L14, 111L3). The same expression is found in singular: xurj’īīin╔Ö <...> /cturnsav╔Ö 
k`c`- [HZ’īīHek` 5, 1966: 647L8]. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Since long (de Lagarde 1854: 26 L689; Dervischjan 1877: 96; Hu’īłbschmann 
1883: 47; 1897: 484), connected with Gr. ’üĘ’éÆ’üŁ’ü»’ü¬ m. `the shoulder with the upper arm', 
Lat. umerus, ’ē¦ m. `shoulder', Skt. a’īüm’īŻsa- m. `shoulder' (RV+), Hitt. ana’åÜ’åÜa- `part of 
the back', Goth. APl amsans `shoulder' etc. [HAB 3: 609b]. 
The loss of *n before *s in Arm. us `shoulder and amis `month' (q.v.) was 
posterior to the development *s > h, to the assibilation of PIE *k^ to *s’īü and to the 
raising of *o to u before a nasal consonant (Kortlandt 1976: 92; 1980: 101 = 2003: 2, 
29; cf. Beekes 2003: 180, 209). It seems impossible to determine whether Arm. us 
continues the full grade as Skt. a’īüm’īŻsa-, or the lengthened grade as Gr. ’üĘ’éÆ’üŁ’ü»’ü¬ [Olsen 
1999: 21]. The vocalism of the Greek is troublesome (see Beekes 1972: 127; 
Nassivera 2000: 6516 with ref.). 
In view of Toch. A es, B a’īäntse `shoulder' probably pointing to *h2emso-, as well 
as the lack of initial aspiration in Arm. us and Hittite ana’åÜ’åÜa-, one reconsructs PIE 
*h2omso- rather than *h3emso- [Lubotsky 1988: 75; Schrijver 1991: 51; Beekes 
2003: 168-169].

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Compared with Lith. kur~ `where' (adv.); IIran. *kwu-tre: Skt. ku’īütra (adv., in 
questions - later) `where, somewhere (indef.)' (RV+), OAv. ku’Åær’É░ (adv.) `where, 
where to', YAv. ku’Åæra `ob wohl (in questions)' (see Hu’īłbschmann 1897: 481; HAB 
3: 613). For discussion of the -r- and related problems see Vanse’īüveren 1995; Hamp 
1997a: 20-21. Viredaz (2005: 85-86) proposes a derivation from PIE *k/wsuperu-dhe 
`where' (interrog.): Skt. ku’īüha `where', OCS kčŖde `where, when', etc. However, the 
development of Arm -r- from intervocalic *dh is uncertain. 
It is better to link the pronominal stem o- `who, etc.' and ur `where' with PIE 
forms with an initial *i’ī»- rather than *kw- (cf. Skt. ya’īü- `who, which' etc.; note Pol. jak 
`how' beside Russ. kak `how'), see Kortlandt 1983: 11; 1997: 7; 1998 = 2003: 41, 
120, 122-124; Weitenberg 1986: 91; Clackson 1994: 52; Beekes 2003: 162-163. 
The final -r in ur > is also found in i-r `thing' and o-r `which' (see Hu’īłbschmann 
1897: 481 and especially HAB 3: 613). That these do not have locative function is 
not a problem since relative and interrogative

title: urd

1898: 98]. For the semantic derivation `wate r(ed)' > `island' see 3.4.2. The affricate

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 3: 616a. The derivation from 
*ud-ro- from the PIE word for `water': Skt. udra’īü- m. `fish otter', YAv. udra- m. 
`otter', Gr. ’üĄ’éō’üż’ü▓’ü»’ü¬ m. `watersnake', ’üĄ’éō’üż’ü▓’üĪ f. `watersnake', OHG ottar `otter', etc. (see 
Dervischjan 1877: 89; ’ēüap`anc`yan 1961: 151-152) is not convincing since the 
expected form is Arm. *urt-. Iranian borrowing is not plausible either since the 
semantics is remote, and e.g. vagr `tiger' and the name Tigran display no metathesis.

urjurjurjurj’īīu’īīu’īīu’īīu, a-stem `stepson or stepdaughter'. 
Attested in Severian of Gabala (GDPl ╔Örj’īīu-ac`), Eusebius of Caesarea. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Bugge (1892: 451; 1893: 23) derives from *ordi-u, composed of ordi 
`generation, son/daughter, espec. son' and the suffix -u as in mawr-u `stepmother'. 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 3: 618-619; 4: 641-642) rejects the connection and derives the word 
from PIE *putro- (cf. Skt. putra’īü- m., Av. pu’Åæra- m. `son, child, young of an animal', 
etc.), treating the -j’īī- as a genitive as in ge/lmidtilde-j’īī (see s.v. giw/lmidtilde `village'), cf. ge/lmidtilde-j’īī-uk 
`peasant'. One misses here the origin of -j’īī-. 
This etymology would become easier if one assumes an i-stem or *-io- suffix (cf. 
*putri’ī»o- mentioned in J’īīahukyan 1987: 186 sceptically), or an original feminine: 
*putr-i(e)h2- > PArm. *u(w)r-j’īī-. The final -u is readily explained as analogical after 
mawr-u `stepmother'. 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 143, 186) accepts Bugge's etymology with reservation and 
considers the other one as less probable. Then he (op. cit. 259-260) points out that 
the PIE origin of urj’īīu is doubtful. The word has been explained as a 
vr’īźddhi-derivative from ordi [Pedersen 1906: 360 = 1982: 138; *Darms 1978: 341; 
Olsen 1989a: 21;

rasxanakertc`i) `proof, argument, reason, 
true cause'. 
Yovhanne’īäs Drasxanakertc`i (9-10th cent.) etc. Earlier and more frequently found 
in compounds: Philo, John Chrysostom, Movse’īäs Ka/lmidtildeankatuac`i, etc. 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymology is accepted in HAB 4: 484a. The connection with Gr. ’ü”’üĪ’Ć» -’ü│’ü®’ü¬ 
f. `utterance, expression; statement; mere assertion, without proof', Lat. fa’īäs `divine 
law; right; obligation', fa’īästi `list of festivals; calendar' etc. from PIE *bheh2- `to 
speak' (J’īīahukyan 1967: 122-123) is problematic both formally and semantically. 
From the semantic point of view, the other Greek ’ü”’üĪ’Ć» -’ü│’ü®’ü¬ , meaning `denunciation, 
information laid; appearance', would match better. Bailey (1986: 7) compares with 
Oss. fa’īäst, fa’īärst(a’īä) `question, counsel', from Iran. fras- `to question' (cf. YAv. fra’åÜna- 
m. `question', Khot. bras’īŻß╣Ła- `questioned' etc.). Neither this is convincing. 
Patruba’īüny (1908: 15 2a) derives Arm. p`ast (i-stem) from QIE *(s)p╔Ök^-ti-, a 
*-ti-derivation from *(s)pek^- `to observe, see', linking with spasem `to wait, serve' 
and asem `to say'. This etymology, though rejected by AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB, ibid.), is 
worth of consideration. 
The PIE root is represented by Skt. (s)pas’īü- `to see (pas’īü-); to observe, to watch, to 
spy ( spas’īü-)', spas’īŻß╣Ła’īü- `(clearly) perceived, clear, visible',

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No acceptable etymology is recorded in HAB 4: 497b. Patruba’īüny (*1908-: 
277b) treats as composed of *(s)bhendh-s (cf. Gr. ’ü░’üź’ü«’ü▒’üź’ü▓’ü»’Ć»’ü¬ etc.) and the ending -ay, 
seen also in car’īćay, i-stem `servant; captive'. J’īīahukyan (1967: 123) repets this 
etymology, but gives it up later (1987: 260), stating that the origin of the word is 
unknown. 
Winter (1966: 203-205) links the word with Lat. procus, i’īä m. `suitor, wooer', 
deriving it from a base *perk^- rather than *prek^- (cf. Lith. čĆąĄr’åÜu `to ask for a girl's 
hand in marriage'), and cites ark`ay `king, ruler' as containing the same suffix; see 
also Gamkrelidze/Ivanov 1984, 1: 237. However, the loss of r (see 2.1.33.3), the 
suffix , and the initial p`- are not clear. The ending -ay is probably somehow related 
with that of yawr-ay `stepfather' (q.v.). Olsen (1999: 946) considers p`esay as a word 
of unknown origin. 
Any relation with Pahl., NPers. pus, pusar, Manich. MPers. pwsr `son'? (see the 
word in MacKenzie 1971: 69). 
On p`esawe’īär see Olsen 1999: 913.

`to distress oneself' [HAB 4: 506b].

J’īīahukyan (1967: 104) proposed a connection with Gr. ’ü░’ü¼’ü©’Ć»’ü│’ü│’üĘ `to beat', ’ü░’ü¼’ü©’ü¦’ü©’Ć» ,

plango’īä `to strike, beat; to beat the breast in mourning, mourn for', Russ. pla’īükat' `to 
cry', etc. This comparison is formally problematic; *plVk/g- and *pl’īźk/g- would yield 
Arm. *lVk`/k- or *ha/lmidtildeK, respectively. The semantic development is perhaps possible 
but not attractive since the Armenian word ba sically refers to the state of bitterness 
or willing to cry rather than to the process of crying. No wonder that J’īīahukyan did 
not include this etymology into his monumental 1987.

p`/lmidtildej-uk and p`/lmidtildej-k-a/im. For the semantics note that the spleen is regarded as the seat

impossible. Later, he (1987: 620) represents the Greek etymology (from ’ü”’ü»’Ć»’ü│’ü│’üĪ , that 
is) with a question-mark. 
One may alternatively consider a comparison with OEngl. furh `Furche, Graben',

*phul(a)- < PArm. *po’īäla-. However, neither PArm. *pa- nor *po’īä- would yield *phV-. 
In order to explain the aspirated stop p`- in the Armenian form, one needs an 
unambiguous sequence *pHV-. The reconstruction of *pho’īäl- (see Pokorny 1959: 
851; J’īīahukyan 1982: 48, 181; 1987: 145) does not help much because, apart from the 
fact that the existence of the PIE series of aspirated voiceless stops is not commonly 
accepted, the Armenian form is the only form suggesting such a stop. An alternative 
*pHo’īäl- is cited in Mallory/Adams (1997: 191b), with a quest

Arm. dial. *k`oli, an isolated and unspecified colour name (see AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913:

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM No etymology is known to me. 
In the passage from P`awstos, Ardeans is said to be a village of the royal treasury 
or, perhaps better, of the royal granary/barn (see s.v. unj’īī2 `treasure, granary'). 
Bearing this in mind, one may derive Ardean-s from Arm. *ard(i), ea-stem `work': 
ardea-w-k` `indeed' (instrumental); ardiwn-k`, APl ardiwn-s, GDPl ardeanc` `deed, 
work; (earth) products' (Bible+), dial. *ard(i)umn `earth goods, harvest' (see s.v. 
ard1). Note that the latter has been preserve d in the dialect of Ararat, which is 
roughly spoken on the eastern part of the province of Ayrarat. Ardean-k` is 
composed of *ardi `work, goods' and the suffix -an-k`, cf. apr-an-k` `products, 
properties' from verbal *apur- `to live, survive'. 
The exact location of Ardean-s is unknown. It is tempting to locate it in S’īīirak, a 
district in Ayrarat, the famous barns of which are mentioned in the old saying 
recorded in Movse’īäs Xorenac`i 1.12 (1913=1991: 40L; transl. Thomson 1978: 90): t`e’īä 
k`o S’īīarayi orkorn e’īä, asen, mer S’īīirakay ambark`n ŌĆ░`en : "If you have the throat of

p`oxanak Dalareac`s anuan "instead of this name of Dalarik`". 
’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Derived from dalar `young, fresh; grass, herbs', dalari `grass, herbs' 
[Hu’īłbschmann 1904: 420]. 
The two names of a place in the passage fr om P`awstos (see above) are treated as 
symbolic and fictitious [Garso/FL872an 1989: 26418, 458]. The symbolic contrast in the 
text is obvious, but this does not necessarily imply that the author made up these 
toponyms. Note that Step`anos Taro’īänec`i/Aso/lmidtildeik (10-11th cent.) has Arjka/lmidtilde-n instead 
of Acu/lmidtilde, though he refers to P`awstos, and Vardan Arewelc`i (13th cent.) - Arcu/lmidtilde-n 
[Hu’īłbschmann 1904: 395]. As for Dalarik`, the appellative dalar(i) `herbs' is a quite 
plausible base to build a toponym upon, and is indeed found in another toponym, viz. 
Dalarink` (in Siwnik`). Furthermore, one may assume that Dalarik` was situated in 
the district of Dalar’īć, bordering with Apahunik` in the north-west, and its name was 
identic with that of the district. I conclude that P`awstos adjusted (one of) the names 
of the village into his symbolic interpreta tion rather than made it/them up. On the 
-r-

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM In "Patmut`iwn srboc` Hr’īćip`simeanc`" (see above), the origin of the 
place-name is traditionally related with the Flood story told among Syrians ( asi

Arche" (cf. Arab. ’ü▒ ama’īänu’īäna `eighty'); compare the modern names of the village: 
Karye i Thma’īänin, i.e. "Dorf der Acht", Kurd. He’åÜta’īäne, i.e. "achtzig" [Hu’īłbschmann 
1904: 333-334]. 
However, this traditional interpretation may be folk-etymological. There are 
variants of the story of Noah's Ark in relation with other montains of the Armenian 
Highland, and these traditional stories too are involved in folk-etymological 
interpretations; cf. NaxŌĆ░-awan, re-interpreted as Nax-ij’īīewan "erste Station" 
[Hu’īłbschmann 1904: 455; 1901: 73-79 = 1990: 99-105] (for the corresponding story 
see ’ēüanalanyan 1969: 157 Nr402); Ar’īćnos as if from *ar’īć (z)Noys "take this Noah!" 
(’ēüanalanyan 1969: 24 Nr51), etc. 
The native Armenian origin of the topony m is not impossible. That the mountains 
of Ararat in the Bible version of the Flood story refer to Armenia is

1989. Remarkably, the etymological semantics of Arm. me/lmidtilde is confirmed by the

more common Armenian name, viz. Me/lmidtilder-a-get, lit. `honey-river', must have been

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Composed of an unclear *o/lmidtilde and the suffix -akan [Hu’īłbschmann 1904: 460]. 
By characterizing the fortress as `rundlich' Tomaschek (1896: 11) probably suggests 
a composition with Arm. 
o’īä/lmidtilde `ring' which would be impossible in view of the vocalic 
difference [Hu’īłbschmann 1904: 460]. 
J’īīahukyan (1987: 416) points out that the suffix -akan (of Parthian origin) occurs 
very seldom with native Armenian stems, and among examples mentions O/lmidtilde-akan. 
He, thus, assumes a native Armenian appellative *o/lmidtilde not specifying it. 
The stem *o/lmidtilde- may be derived from Arm. o/lmidtilde(n) `spine, back' which in dialects 
(including Mu’åÜ, located on the same territory of Taro’īän) refers to `slope of a 
mountain', `long hillock',
 `the upper part of a hill' (see s.v.).

’äć’äć’äć’äć ETYM Clearly composed as tuarac + -a- + tap` `plain, land'. The word tuarac (see 
s.v. tuar `cattle') means `pasturing' (Eusebius of Caesarea: i tuaraci) and `pasturer, 
herdsman' (in a homily of / ascribed to E/lmidtildei’åÜe’īä), cf. also tuarac-akan `herdsman' 
(Bible+) [NHB 2: 890bc]. The place-name has been explained in NHB (2: 890c) as 
"a plain place of pasturing" ( te/lmidtildei aro’īäti tap`arak). Hu’īłbschmann (1904: 476), however, 
departs from the meaning `herdsman' (`Hirt') and interprets the place-name as 
`Hirtenebene' (for the component tap` see ibid. 388). The same view is reflected in 
Kapancjan 1940, V. XaŌĆ░atrjan 1980: 111. Note that only the meaning `herdsman' is 
present in dialects (s ee AŌĆ░ar’īćean 1913: 1019b). For `pasturer' > `pasturing' see also 
s.v. hawran. 
AŌĆ░ar’īćyan (HAB 4: 424a) points out that tuarac-a-tap` `place for cattle pasturing' 
also (underlining mine - HM) appears as a place-name. In fact, there seems to exist 
no attestation for this compounded appellative. NHB (2: 860c, 890c) cites one 
illustration found in Aristake’īäs Lastivertc`i 16 (11th cent.): i tuara
